title: Detecting vortex motion through spatially correlated nonequilibrium noise
summary: Resistive transport near a superconducting phase can arise from the motion of normal-state quasiparticles or that of vortices. The conductivity alone does not distinguish between these mechanisms. We propose an unambiguous method for telling them apart, using the recently developed experimental tool of covariance magnetometry, which uses nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond to probe real-time spatiotemporal correlations in magnetic noise. Our key insight is that, under an applied current, the underlying charge carriers leave a directional fingerprint in the spatially correlated magnetic noise above the sample: ordinary electric carriers drift parallel to the current, whereas vortices, owing to the Magnus force, drift perpendicular to it. The noise covariance detects this anisotropy and identifies the vortex-driven nature of transport. We compute the noise correlations expected for a representative thin-film superconductor and demonstrate that the anisotropic signal is well within the reach of current experimental capabilities.

AI.tldr: A novel method using NV-center covariance magnetometry to distinguish between quasiparticle and vortex-driven resistive transport in superconductors by detecting directional anisotropies in magnetic noise correlations.
AI.motivation: In superconductors near the superconducting phase, resistive transport can arise from either normal-state quasiparticle motion or vortex motion. Conventional conductivity measurements cannot distinguish between these two mechanisms, creating ambiguity in understanding the transport properties.
AI.method: The authors propose using covariance magnetometry with nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond to probe real-time spatiotemporal correlations in magnetic noise above a thin-film superconductor. Under an applied current, ordinary charge carriers drift parallel to the current direction, while vortices drift perpendicular to the current due to the Magnus force. These different drift directions create distinct anisotropic signatures in the spatially correlated magnetic noise that can be detected.
AI.result: The noise correlations were computed for a representative thin-film superconductor, demonstrating that the anisotropic signal resulting from vortex-driven versus quasiparticle-driven transport is well within the detection capabilities of current experimental setups.
AI.conclusion: Covariance magnetometry provides an unambiguous experimental tool to differentiate between quasiparticle and vortex-driven resistive transport in superconductors by detecting the directional fingerprint in magnetic noise correlations.

title: Signatures of Gaussian superconducting fluctuations in nonlocal noise magnetometry
summary: We calculate the two-point magnetic noise spectrum arising from Gaussian superconducting fluctuations, a quantity directly measurable by spin qubit pairs such as nitrogen vacancy centers in diamond. The analysis utilizes the time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau theory, reflecting the direct contribution of fluctuating Cooper pairs to the current correlations and consequent magnetic noise. We treat both two-dimensional systems and wires, considering them in equilibrium and under a uniform electric field. The signal is expected to be strongest in high-temperature superconductors, and we contrast our findings with the predicted signatures of a vortex liquid to offer an additional route to elucidate the nature of fluctuations in these systems.

AI.tldr: Calculates the magnetic noise spectrum from Gaussian superconducting fluctuations using time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau theory, showing this noise is measurable by NV centers and strongest in high-Tc superconductors, providing a method to distinguish different fluctuation regimes.
AI.motivation: To develop a direct measurable signature of superconducting fluctuations that can probe the nature of these fluctuations in high-temperature superconductors using experimentally accessible spin qubit probes (NV centers).
AI.method: Uses time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau theory to calculate current correlations from fluctuating Cooper pairs, treating 2D systems and wires in equilibrium and under electric field.
AI.result: A calculable two-point magnetic noise spectrum arising from Gaussian superconducting fluctuations that is directly measurable and distinguishable from vortex liquid signatures.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a new experimental route to probe superconducting fluctuations using magnetic noise measurements with NV centers, offering a way to distinguish between Gaussian fluctuations and vortex liquid phases in high-temperature superconductors.

title: Nearly perfect Fermi surface nesting in hole-doped La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ enables bulk superconductivity without pressure or strain
summary: The discovery of high-temperature superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates has drawn great attention. However, unlike cuprates and iron-based superconductors, Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates exhibit superconductivity either under high pressure in bulk samples or under compressive strain in thin films. Genuine bulk superconductivity under ambient pressure has remained elusive in these materials, precluding key measurements such as specific heat and superfluid density. In this work, we combine density-functional-theory, dynamical-mean-field-theory, and random-phase-approximation to solve the superconducting gap equation for bulk hole-doped bilayer nickelate La$_{3-x}$Sr$_x$Ni$_2$O$_7$ at ambient pressure. We find that hole doping induces a Ni-$d_{3z^2-r^2}$-derived $γ$ pocket on the Fermi surface, and serves as a tuning parameter for both its size and \textit{shape}. As $x$ approaches 0.4, the $γ$ pocket evolves from circular to diamond-shaped and expands to span half of the Brillouin zone, resulting in nearly perfect Fermi surface nesting with the optimal nesting vector $\textbf{Q} = (π, π)$. This, in turn, strongly enhances antiferromagnetic spin fluctuations and substantially increases the leading superconducting eigenvalue to a level at which superconductivity becomes experimentally observable. Our work provides both a robust mechanism and an experimentally feasible route to inducing the long-sought bulk superconductivity in La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ without pressure or strain.

AI.tldr: This study uses DFT+DMFT+RPA calculations to show that hole doping in La3-xSrxNi2O7 creates a γ pocket that, at x≈0.4, enables near-perfect Fermi surface nesting and enhances spin fluctuations sufficiently to achieve bulk superconductivity at ambient pressure—solving a key problem in nickelate superconductors.
AI.motivation: The discovery of high-temperature superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates has generated great interest. However, unlike cuprates and iron-based superconductors, these materials only exhibit superconductivity under high pressure (bulk) or compressive strain (thin films). Genuine bulk superconductivity at ambient pressure has remained elusive, which has prevented key measurements like specific heat and superfluid density.
AI.method: The researchers combined density-functional-theory (DFT), dynamical-mean-field-theory (DMFT), and random-phase-approximation (RPA) to solve the superconducting gap equation for bulk hole-doped bilayer nickelate La3-xSrxNi2O7 at ambient pressure.
AI.result: Hole doping induces a Ni-d3z2-r2-derived γ pocket on the Fermi surface. As x approaches 0.4, the γ pocket evolves from circular to diamond-shaped and expands to span half of the Brillouin zone, resulting in nearly perfect Fermi surface nesting with optimal nesting vector Q=(π,π). This strongly enhances antiferromagnetic spin fluctuations and substantially increases the leading superconducting eigenvalue to experimentally observable levels.
AI.conclusion: This work provides both a robust mechanism and an experimentally feasible route to inducing long-sought bulk superconductivity in La3Ni2O7 without applying external pressure or strain.

title: The Ultrafast Superconducting Diode Effect
summary: Nonreciprocal transport is generally observed in superconductors in which time reversal and inversion symmetries are simultaneously broken. This effect, which may become one of the backbones for future superconducting electronics, arises because of asymmetric vortex transport in a magnetic field. However, vortex transport is also intrinsically dissipative and limited in speed. Here, we report on the discovery of ultrafast non-reciprocal transport in centrosymmetric superconductors. For NbN films biased with a quasi-DC supercurrent, picosecond current pulses with the same sign as the bias experience resistive impedance, whereas pulses of opposite polarity encounter an inductive response. Strikingly, the effect is at least three orders of magnitude faster than in conventional superconducting diodes, limited only by ultrafast current-induced depairing. We demonstrate rectification of a 100 GHz signal, with dissipation levels of a few fJ per cycle. We foresee potential for superconducting logic elements, operating at THz bit rates with aJ energy dissipation per operation.

AI.tldr: Discovery of ultrafast non-reciprocal transport in centrosymmetric superconductors (NbN films) using quasi-DC supercurrent bias, achieving rectification of 100 GHz signals with minimal dissipation.
AI.motivation: Conventional nonreciprocal transport in superconductors relies on vortex transport which is intrinsically dissipative and slow, limiting applications in superconducting electronics. The authors seek to discover a faster, more efficient alternative mechanism.
AI.method: NbN films were biased with a quasi-DC supercurrent, then tested with picosecond current pulses of both same and opposite polarities relative to the bias. Electrical response was measured to determine impedance characteristics.
AI.result: Same-sign pulses experience resistive impedance while opposite-polarity pulses show inductive response. The effect is >1000 times faster than conventional superconducting diodes, limited only by ultrafast current-induced depairing. Demonstrated 100 GHz signal rectification with only a few fJ dissipation per cycle.
AI.conclusion: This discovery enables potential superconducting logic elements operating at THz bit rates with aJ energy dissipation per operation, representing a major advancement for superconducting electronics.

title: Imaging stripe dynamics in trilayer nickelate La$_4$Ni$_3$O$_{10}$
summary: Since the discovery of high-temperature superconductivity in nickelate superconductors, it is an open question how closely the superconducting state resembles that of cuprate superconductors. One salient feature of the phase diagram of the high-temperature cuprate superconductors is stripe order. Despite their prevalence, real-space imaging has been limited to the charge sector. Here we use spin-polarised scanning tunnelling microscopy to visualize the local magnetic and charge distribution emerging due to a stripe order in the trilayer nickelate La$_4$Ni$_3$O$_{10}$. The stripe order exhibits a four unit cell periodicity, closely resembling that seen in cuprates, and opens a near-complete $\sim66\mathrm{meV}$ gap at the Fermi level. Crucially, discrete phase slips can be triggered by tunneling electrons above a $\sim 20\mathrm{meV}$ threshold, allowing imaging of stripe dynamics at the atomic scale. These results highlight the importance of correlation physics driving stripe-like orders in lanthanum nickelates with striking similarities to the cuprates.

AI.tldr: This study uses spin-polarized STM to investigate stripe order in trilayer nickelate La₄Ni₃O₁₀, revealing a 4-unit-cell periodic stripe order similar to cuprates, with a ~66 meV gap and observable dynamics via phase slips above ~20 meV threshold.
AI.motivation: To address the open question of how closely the superconducting state in nickelate superconductors resembles that of cuprate superconductors, particularly regarding stripe order - a key feature in cuprate phase diagrams.
AI.method: Spin-polarized scanning tunneling microscopy was employed to visualize the local magnetic and charge distribution arising from stripe order in the trilayer nickelate La₄Ni₃O₁₀.
AI.result: The stripe order exhibits a four unit cell periodicity, closely resembling cuprates, and opens a near-complete ~66 meV gap at the Fermi level. Discrete phase slips can be triggered by tunneling electrons above a ~20 meV threshold, enabling atomic-scale imaging of stripe dynamics.
AI.conclusion: These findings highlight the importance of correlation physics in driving stripe-like orders in lanthanum nickelates, demonstrating striking similarities to cuprate superconductors.

title: Atomistic Modeling of Chemical Disorder in Materials: Bridging Classical Methods and AI-Assisted Approaches
summary: Chemical disorder, originating from the mixed occupation of crystallographic sites by multiple elements, is widespread in alloys, ceramics, and compositionally complex materials, where short- and long-range orderings can strongly influence properties. A central obstacle is the representation gap between experiments and simulations: experiments often report disorder as partial occupancies and ensemble-averaged behaviors, whereas atomistic simulations and AI workflows usually require fully specified configurations. Tackling this gap requires computational methods that convert averaged disorder descriptions into representative configurational ensembles while balancing cost, bias, and fidelity. This challenge has become more urgent in AI-driven computational discovery, where ignoring disorder may cause AI workflows to misrank stability, misjudge novelty, and misdirect experiments with too-idealized representations. This Review highlights how classical and AI-driven methods can bridge this representation gap. We assess the strengths and limitations of approaches spanning mean-field theories, cluster expansion, quasi-random approximations, Monte Carlo, and emerging schemes powered by universal interatomic potentials and generative models. We further highlight how AI can accelerate classical computational schemes by lowering the cost of microstate evaluation, configurational exploration, and atomistic-to-thermodynamic closure. We also emphasize how AI can enable disorder-native capabilities, including workflow triage, ordering-sensitive and alchemical representations, generative models of disordered structures and distributions, and kinetics-aware disorder prediction. Together, this framework outlines a practical roadmap toward disorder-native AI, which can transform chemical disorder from a representational obstacle into a controllable variable for realistic AI-accelerated materials discovery.

AI.tldr: A review paper addressing the computational challenge of representing chemical disorder (mixed occupation of crystallographic sites) in materials, bridging the gap between experimental averaged descriptions and atomistic simulation/AI requirements. The paper covers classical methods (mean-field theory, cluster expansion, Monte Carlo) and AI-driven approaches (generative models, universal potentials), proposing a roadmap toward "disorder-native AI" for materials discovery.
AI.motivation: Chemical disorder widely exists in alloys, ceramics, and complex materials, strongly influencing material properties through short- and long-range orderings. However, a critical representation gap exists: experiments report disorder as partial occupancies and ensemble averages, while atomistic simulations and AI workflows require fully specified configurations. This gap causes AI workflows to misrank stability, misjudge novelty, and misdirect experiments with overly idealized representations, making the challenge increasingly urgent in AI-driven computational discovery.
AI.method: The paper reviews and assesses multiple approaches spanning: (1) Mean-field theories, (2) Cluster expansion, (3) Quasi-random approximations, (4) Monte Carlo methods, (5) Universal interatomic potentials, and (6) Emerging generative models. It explores how AI can accelerate classical computational schemes by lowering computational costs and enabling disorder-native capabilities including workflow triage, ordering-sensitive representations, generative models of disordered structures, and kinetics-aware disorder prediction.
AI.result: The paper provides a comprehensive assessment of strengths and limitations for each method category. It outlines a practical framework and roadmap toward "disorder-native AI," which transforms chemical disorder from a representational obstacle into a controllable variable for realistic AI-accelerated materials discovery.
AI.conclusion: Successfully bridging the representation gap between experimental disorder descriptions and computational requirements is essential for accurate AI-driven materials discovery. The combination of classical methods enhanced by AI capabilities, along with emerging disorder-native AI approaches, offers a promising pathway to handle chemical disorder realistically, enabling better stability ranking, novelty assessment, and experimental guidance.

title: Secondary Collective Excitations in Intermediate to Strong-Coupling Superconductors
summary: Considering systematically derived energy-transfer-dependent effective electron-electron interactions leads to the appearance of secondary phase and amplitude modes in isotropic superconductors in the intermediate-to-strong-coupling regime. We study the implications of such interactions on Bravais lattices by computing the corresponding response functions using the iterated equations of motion (iEoM) approach. In the weak-coupling regime, we find the conventional, primary amplitude and phase modes at $ω=2Δ$ and $ω=0$, respectively. For intermediate coupling, the amplitude mode detaches from the quasiparticle continuum towards lower energies. Increasing the coupling further leads to additional, long-lived secondary collective excitations below the continuum. This phenomenon is largely independent of the underlying lattice and the specific Fermi level. The amplitude and phase modes couple if the system is not particle-hole symmetric. Additionally, we extend the method to compute eigenoperators, i.e., linear combinations of operators that excite each secondary mode specifically. We identify nodal structures in the coefficients for these eigenoperators reminiscent of wave functions in the Hydrogen problem.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates energy-transfer-dependent electron-electron interactions in isotropic superconductors, revealing secondary amplitude and phase collective modes in the intermediate-to-strong coupling regime that appear below the quasiparticle continuum, using the iterated equations of motion (iEoM) approach.
AI.motivation: To understand how systematic derivations of energy-transfer-dependent effective electron-electron interactions affect collective excitations in superconductors beyond the conventional weak-coupling regime, particularly regarding secondary modes that have not been previously characterized.
AI.method: Using the iterated equations of motion (iEoM) approach to compute response functions for various Bravais lattices, investigating different coupling regimes from weak to intermediate to strong coupling, and extending the method to compute eigenoperators that specifically excite each secondary mode.
AI.result: In weak-coupling, conventional primary amplitude mode at ω=2Δ and phase mode at ω=0 are found. In intermediate coupling, the amplitude mode detaches from the quasiparticle continuum toward lower energies. Further increasing coupling reveals additional long-lived secondary collective excitations below the continuum. These features are largely independent of lattice structure and Fermi level. Without particle-hole symmetry, amplitude and phase modes couple. Nodal structures in eigenoperator coefficients resemble wavefunctions in the Hydrogen problem.
AI.conclusion: Secondary collective modes emerge systematically in the intermediate-to-strong coupling regime due to energy-transfer-dependent interactions, representing a general feature of superconductors that extends beyond specific lattice geometries. The methodology provides a framework for computing eigenoperators that target specific excited states, with applications in understanding collective excitations in strongly coupled superconductors.

title: Magnetic phases in the $J_{1}$-$J_{2}$ antiferromagnetic XY model on the honeycomb lattice
summary: We study ground-state properties and phase diagram of the $J_{1}$-$J_{2}$ antiferromagnetic XY model on the honeycomb lattice by means of the developed corner transfer matrix renormalization group algorithm with the two-site unit cell and the infinite spiral projected entangled pair states ansatz. We identify the main phases: Néel, Ising, collinear, and incommensurate spiral phases, as well as the transitions between them, as functions of the ratio $J_{2}/J_{1}$. In the regime of competing types of ordering, we show that the energies of the dimerized states are systematically higher than the energies in the collinear phase. This collinear phase transforms to the incommensurate spiral phase through the second-order phase transition upon a further increase of $J_2/J_1$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the J1-J2 antiferromagnetic XY model on the honeycomb lattice using advanced numerical techniques (CTMRG and iPEPS). It maps out the phase diagram identifying Néel, Ising, collinear, and incommensurate spiral phases, and characterizes the phase transitions between them as a function of J2/J1 ratio.
AI.motivation: Understanding the ground-state properties and phase diagram of competing antiferromagnetic interactions on the honeycomb lattice is important for quantum Magnetism. The J1-J2 model exhibits rich physics due to frustration, where competing interactions lead to multiple ordered phases and phase transitions.
AI.method: The authors employ two complementary numerical methods: (1) Corner Transfer Matrix Renormalization Group (CTMRG) with two-site unit cell, and (2) Infinite Spiral Projected Entangled Pair States (iPEPS) ansatz. These are state-of-the-art techniques for studying 2D quantum lattice models.
AI.result: The study identifies four main phases: Néel, Ising, collinear, and incommensurate spiral phases. The phase transitions between these phases as functions of J2/J1 are characterized. Additionally, the authors find that dimerized states have systematically higher energies than the collinear phase in the regime of competing ordering.
AI.conclusion: The collinear phase transforms to the incommensurate spiral phase through a second-order phase transition upon increasing J2/J1. This work provides a comprehensive phase diagram of the J1-J2 XY model on the honeycomb lattice using advanced tensor network methods.

title: Frequency-dependent stress response under thermal cycle: A thermal-crystal plasticity and dynamic mode decomposition study
comment: Accepted for publication in International Journal of Plasticity
summary: Thermal cycle environments involving repeated temperature changes are common conditions observed in modern engineering processes. Under such conditions, materials undergo repeated thermal expansion and contraction, forming complex thermal stress fields. Thermal-crystal plasticity simulations that account for stress fields and thermal conduction at the polycrystalline microstructure scale are an effective method for numerically reproducing thermal cycle environments. However, the influence of thermal cycle frequency on the temporal behavior of the stress field and plastic response has not yet been fully understood, partly because a systematic analysis method capable of simultaneously capturing spatial heterogeneity and temporal evolution remains limited. In this study, we predicted the thermal stress field generated under different thermal cycle frequencies using thermal-crystal plasticity simulations and investigated the effect of frequency on the spatiotemporal structure of the stress response. The present framework illustrates that the resulting thermal-mechanical response can be represented as a superposition of multiple effective temporal components, reflecting the increased complexity of the system behavior. By employing dynamic mode decomposition (DMD) as a diagnostic and post-processing technique, we demonstrate that the spatiotemporal structure of the stress field under thermal cycle conditions can be systematically extracted and compactly represented. This approach enables a quantitative characterization of frequency-dependent changes in the thermal stress response beyond conventional averaging or snapshot-based analyses. The results highlight the utility of DMD as a framework for organizing complex simulation data and for interpreting the temporal structure of plastic response under cyclic thermal loading.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the effect of thermal cycle frequency on thermal stress fields in materials using thermal-crystal plasticity simulations combined with Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD) for spatiotemporal analysis. The study demonstrates that DMD can effectively extract and represent frequency-dependent stress response characteristics beyond conventional averaging methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from a gap in understanding how thermal cycle frequency affects the temporal behavior of stress fields and plastic responses in materials. Existing methods lack the capability to simultaneously capture spatial heterogeneity and temporal evolution under repeated thermal cycling conditions encountered in engineering processes.
AI.method: The methodology involves thermal-crystal plasticity simulations that account for stress fields and thermal conduction at the polycrystalline microstructure scale. Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD) is employed as both a diagnostic and post-processing technique to extract spatiotemporal structures from the simulation data, enabling characterization of frequency-dependent changes in the thermal stress response.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates that the thermal-mechanical response under thermal cycling can be represented as a superposition of multiple effective temporal components, reflecting increased complexity of system behavior. DMD successfully extracted and compactly represented the spatiotemporal structure of stress fields, providing quantitative characterization of frequency-dependent changes that conventional snapshot-based analyses cannot capture.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that DMD serves as an effective framework for organizing complex simulation data and interpreting the temporal structure of plastic response under cyclic thermal loading. This approach enables systematic analysis of frequency effects on thermal stress responses beyond traditional averaging or snapshot-based methods.

title: Deconfined Boundary Phase Transition of a Quantum Critical Heisenberg Model
summary: We investigate the boundary phases of a (2+1)-dimensional quantum critical Heisenberg model with a dangling spin chain. By introducing a multispin $Q$-term along the boundary, we drive a continuous boundary transition from an antiferromagnetic (AF) order to a valence-bond solid (VBS) order. Using large-scale quantum Monte Carlo simulations, we locate the critical point at $Q_{c}=0.310(11)$, and obtain the critical exponents at $Q_{c}$, including $y_{s}=0.81(4)$ and the scaling dimensions of AF and VBS order parameters $Δ_{s}=0.660(15)$ and $Δ_{v}=0.204(14)$. The weak long-range AF order for $Q<Q_{c}$ is stabilized by quasi-long-range effective interactions mediated by the critical bulk state, while the VBS phase restores the ordinary critical behavior. Our findings highlight the synergy between topological terms and quasi-long-range interactions in low-dimensional quantum many-body systems.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates boundary phase transitions in a (2+1)-dimensional quantum critical Heisenberg model with a dangling spin chain using quantum Monte Carlo simulations. The study discovers a continuous boundary transition between antiferromagnetic (AF) and valence-bond solid (VBS) orders driven by a multispin Q-term, locating the critical point at Q_c = 0.310(11) and identifying critical exponents.
AI.motivation: Understanding the interplay between topological terms and quasi-long-range interactions in low-dimensional quantum many-body systems remains a challenging problem. The boundary transitions between different ordered phases in these systems had not been thoroughly characterized, particularly the synergy between topological terms and effective interactions mediated by critical bulk states.
AI.method: Large-scale quantum Monte Carlo simulations were performed on a (2+1)-dimensional quantum critical Heisenberg model with a dangling spin chain. A multispin Q-term was introduced along the boundary to drive the transition. The critical point was located at Q_c = 0.310(11), and critical exponents including y_s = 0.81(4), Δ_s = 0.660(15), and Δ_v = 0.204(14) were obtained.
AI.result: The critical point was precisely located at Q_c = 0.310(11). For Q < Q_c, weak long-range AF order is stabilized by quasi-long-range effective interactions mediated by the critical bulk state. For Q > Q_c, the system exhibits VBS order that restores ordinary critical behavior. The scaling dimensions of AF and VBS order parameters were measured as Δ_s = 0.660(15) and Δ_v = 0.204(14), respectively.
AI.conclusion: The findings demonstrate the importance of the synergy between topological terms (like the Q-term) and quasi-long-range interactions in determining the critical behavior of boundary phases. This highlights how topological perturbations can fundamentally alter the nature of ordering in low-dimensional quantum many-body systems.

title: Cryogenically Enhanced Laser-Induced Amorphous Phase Transitions in Crystalline Silicon
summary: Amorphization of silicon is crucial to applications in photonics, microelectronics and solar cell technologies. Ultrafast lasers have been used to generate amorphous silicon from crystalline silicon using rapid nonthermal melting and solidification in room temperature. As material temperature can affect cooling rates significantly, adding temperature control in ultrafast laser modification of silicon may allow a new degree of freedom in ultrafast laser modification. In this work, we investigate the role of cryogenic temperature in governing ultrafast damage pathways via single-shot femtosecond laser irradiation of silicon from room temperature down to 24K at 1030nm. Across this temperature range, we observe a pronounced enhancement of amorphization at lower temperatures, revealed through optical microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM). Raman analysis identifies this ring as an amorphous surface layer, while complementary AFM and SEM imaging show temperature-dependent changes in surface morphology, including localized melt redistribution and refrozen material. To elucidate the physical origins of this behavior, we implement a carrier dependent two-temperature model (nTTM). The simulations reproduce the experimentally observed trends and indicate that reduced phonon population, modified absorption pathways, and altered lattice relaxation dynamics at cryogenic temperatures collectively promote amorphous freezing over recrystallization. This study represents the first detailed examination of silicon under ultrafast irradiation below the liquid-nitrogen regime and reveals temperature-governed mechanisms relevant for advanced silicon microstructuring.

AI.tldr: Ultrafast femtosecond laser irradiation of silicon at cryogenic temperatures (24K- room temperature) enhances amorphization, with lower temperatures promoting amorphous phase formation over recrystallization due to reduced phonon population and modified lattice dynamics.
AI.motivation: Amorphization of silicon is important for photonics, microelectronics, and solar cell applications. While ultrafast lasers can create amorphous silicon via nonthermal melting, temperature effects on the process remain unexplored. This study aims to add temperature control as a new degree of freedom in ultrafast laser modification, specifically investigating cryogenic temperatures below the liquid-nitrogen regime for the first time.
AI.method: Single-shot femtosecond laser irradiation at 1030nm was performed on silicon samples at temperatures ranging from room temperature down to 24K. Material characterization used optical microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM), AFM, and SEM imaging. Theoretical analysis employed a carrier-dependent two-temperature model (nTTM) to simulate the underlying physical mechanisms.
AI.result: A pronounced enhancement of amorphization was observed at lower temperatures across the 24K to room temperature range. Raman spectroscopy confirmed an amorphous surface layer formation. Surface morphology showed temperature-dependent changes including localized melt redistribution and refrozen material. The nTTM simulations successfully reproduced the experimental trends.
AI.conclusion: Reduced phonon population, modified absorption pathways, and altered lattice relaxation dynamics at cryogenic temperatures collectively promote amorphous freezing over recrystallization. This study provides the first detailed examination of silicon under ultrafast irradiation below the liquid-nitrogen regime and reveals temperature-governed mechanisms relevant for advanced silicon microstructuring applications.

title: Hidden weak-pairing superconductivity of non-interacting anyons obeying $\frac{1}{3}$ statistics
summary: We show that a non-interacting gas of charge-$e/3$ anyons with exchange statistics $θ=-π/3$ can superconduct through a hidden weak-pairing mechanism. Such an anyon gas arises naturally in doped fractional Chern insulators at filling $1/3$ or $2/3$, where projective lattice translations enforce three degenerate anyon pockets. Exploiting this three-pocket structure, we develop a flux-attachment construction in which the average statistical flux vanishes, thereby mapping the problem to three species of composite fermions (CFs) in zero effective magnetic field. We show that the anyon statistics itself, encoded in statistical gauge field fluctuations, supplies the pairing glue and drives the CFs into a $p-\mathrm{i}p$ paired state, which corresponds to a $f-\mathrm{i}f$ physical superconductor. The CF strong-pairing phase is adiabatically connected to Laughlin's picture of anyon superconductivity, where charge-$e/3$ anyons bind into charge-$2e/3$ molecules, which then lead to superconductivity. By contrast, the more natural weak-pairing phase of CFs realizes a distinct superconducting phase - its edge is characterized by a chiral central charge $c_-=-1/2$, in contrast to the prediction of integer $c_-$ for the anyon superconductor based on Laughlin's picture, thereby resolving the discrepancy between previous theories and recent numerical results. Our theory provides a natural framework for understanding superconductivity near fractional Chern insulators, as observed in recent experiments. Finally, we discuss extensions of our theory that predict new chiral superconductors adjacent to FCIs at other fillings.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that non-interacting charge-e/3 anyons with θ = -π/3 in doped fractional Chern insulators can superconduct via a hidden weak-pairing mechanism. Using a flux-attachment construction with three composite fermion species, they demonstrate that anyon statistics itself provides pairing glue, yielding a p-ip paired state corresponding to f-if physical superconductors with c_- = -1/2. This resolves discrepancies between earlier theories and numerical simulations.
AI.motivation: This work addresses the discrepancy between previous theories of anyon superconductivity based on Laughlin's picture (which predicted integer chiral central charge) and recent numerical results showing half-integer values. Additionally, it aims to explain superconductivity observed in experiments near fractional Chern insulators.
AI.method: The authors developed a flux-attachment construction exploiting the three-pocket structure of anyons in doped fractional Chern insulators at filling 1/3 or 2/3. They mapped the problem to three species of composite fermions in zero effective magnetic field, showing that anyon statistics itself provides the pairing glue, driving the composite fermions into a p-ip paired state corresponding to a f-if physical superconductor.
AI.result: They demonstrated that charge-e/3 anyons can superconduct through a hidden weak-pairing mechanism distinct from Laughlin's strong-pairing phase. The weak-pairing phase has a chiral central charge c_- = -1/2 (versus integer c_- in the strong-pairing phase), resolving the discrepancy with numerical results. This phase is adiabatically connected to Laughlin's picture but represents a distinct superconducting phase.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a theoretical framework explaining superconductivity near fractional Chern insulators, predicting new chiral superconductors at various fillings. It resolves the discrepancy between previous theories (predicting integer chiral central charge) and numerical results by proposing a weak-pairing phase of composite fermions with c_- = -1/2.

title: Charge Symmetry Beyond Wyckoff Equivalence
summary: Crystallographic symmetry is usually taken as a guide to electronic equivalence in crystals: atoms on the same Wyckoff position are expected to have the same charge, whereas atoms on different Wyckoff positions are expected to be electronically distinct. Here we show that both expectations can fail in oppo-site ways: crystallographically equivalent sites can become charge-inequivalent under compression, whereas crystallographically inequivalent sites can remain charge-equivalent at low pressure because of an emergent hidden symmetry. We develop a minimal Landau theory of pressure-induced charge transfer, in which compression enhances the intersite Coulomb energy gained by charge redistribution until it overcomes the onsite charging cost and destabilizes the charge-equivalent state. In BCC Na, all sites are charge-equivalent at low pressure, but compression drives charge transfer between neighboring sites, pro-ducing an electronically symmetry-broken CsCl-type state on an unchanged BCC ionic framework. In hP4 Na, the opposite anomaly occurs: two Na sites occupy distinct Wyckoff positions, yet remain charge-equivalent at low pressure because of an emergent gauge equivalence in the low-energy manifold, giving rise to near-Fermi doublets that appear accidental in conventional space-group analysis. Upon compres-sion, pressure-induced charge transfer breaks this hidden equivalence, splits the near-Fermi doublets, and drives a metal-insulator transition. These two complementary cases establish pressure-induced charge transfer as a mechanism by which electronic equivalence can either fall below or rise above what Wyckoff positions alone would suggest, showing that lattice symmetry constrains but does not uniquely determine the equivalence structure of the electronic state.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how crystallographic symmetry relates to electronic equivalence in sodium under pressure, challenging the assumption that Wyckoff positions uniquely determine charge distribution. Two complementary cases are studied: in BCC Na, compression drives charge transfer between crystallographically equivalent sites, while in hP4 Na, crystallographically distinct sites remain charge-equivalent at low pressure due to hidden symmetry.
AI.motivation: The traditional view assumes that atoms on the same Wyckoff position have the same charge, while those on different Wyckoff positions are electronically distinct. This work challenges both assumptions by showing that compression can make equivalent sites charge-inequivalent, and that inequivalent sites can remain charge-equivalent through emergent hidden symmetry.
AI.method: The authors developed a minimal Landau theory of pressure-induced charge transfer, analyzing how compression affects intersite Coulomb energy versus onsite charging cost. They studied BCC Na and hP4 Na phases under compression to understand electronic equivalence structures.
AI.result: In BCC Na: All sites are charge-equivalent at low pressure, but compression drives charge transfer between neighbors, producing an electronically symmetry-broken CsCl-type state on unchanged BCC framework. In hP4 Na: Two distinct Wyckoff sites remain charge-equivalent at low pressure due to emergent gauge equivalence, creating near-Fermi doublets. Compression breaks this hidden equivalence, splits the doublets, and drives a metal-insulator transition.
AI.conclusion: Pressure-induced charge transfer is established as a mechanism where electronic equivalence can fall below or rise above what Wyckoff positions alone would suggest. Lattice symmetry constrains but does not uniquely determine the equivalence structure of the electronic state.

title: Itinerant Nature of Spin-Density-Wave Order in Ruddlesden-Popper Nickelates
summary: The nature of magnetism in layered Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates remains a central open question, particularly in light of recent observations of spin-wave-like magnetic excitations in metallic multilayer compounds. Here, we develop a unified itinerant description of spin-density-wave (SDW) order and magnetic excitations in La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ and La$_4$Ni$_3$O$_{10}$. The essential ingredient is the multilayer mirror structure of the NiO$_2$ blocks, which organizes the low-energy electronic states into mirror-even and mirror-odd sectors. We show that dominant interband nesting between mirror-opposite bands drives a mirror-selective itinerant SDW instability, whose collective modes naturally reproduce the experimentally observed spin-wave-like spectra. In La$_4$Ni$_3$O$_{10}$, the SDW further induces a secondary mirror-even charge density wave, yielding intertwined spin and charge textures. Our results demonstrate that magnetism in multilayer nickelates is fundamentally itinerant rather than local-moment in origin, and establish mirror-selective interband SDW order as a unifying organizing principle for magnetic correlations in these systems.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a unified itinerant description of magnetism in layered Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates (La₃Ni₂O₇ and La₄Ni₃O₁₀), demonstrating that the magnetism originates from itinerant spin-density-wave (SDW) instabilities rather than local moment physics.
AI.motivation: The nature of magnetism in layered Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates was an open question, especially after recent observations of spin-wave-like magnetic excitations in metallic multilayer compounds. Previous theories largely relied on local-moment interpretations, but this failed to explain the experimental observations comprehensively.
AI.method: The authors developed a unified itinerant description using the multilayer mirror structure of NiO₂ blocks, which organizes low-energy electronic states into mirror-even and mirror-odd sectors. They analyzed interband nesting between mirror-opposite bands to drive the SDW instability.
AI.result: Dominant interband nesting between mirror-opposite bands drives a mirror-selective itinerant SDW instability. The collective modes naturally reproduce the experimentally observed spin-wave-like spectra. In La₄Ni₃O₁₀, the SDW further induces a secondary mirror-even charge density wave, yielding intertwined spin and charge textures.
AI.conclusion: Magnetism in multilayer nickelates is fundamentally itinerant rather than local-moment in origin. Mirror-selective interband SDW order serves as a unifying organizing principle for magnetic correlations in these systems.

title: The analysis of heat capacity of MnGe metallic helimagnet
summary: Zero-field heat capacity of metallic helimagnet MnGe was analyzed based on the results of resistivity decomposition published previously by our group for the same crystal. Current procedure allowed identifying along with ($i$) electronic ($\tildeγ$ $\approx$ 7 mJ/mol$\cdot$K$^2$) and ($ii$) phononic ($Θ_D$ $\approx$ 350 K) components ($iii$) the additional term, caused by the presence of spin fluctuations (SFs). The last contribution was found to exist in a wide range of temperatures in both paramagnetic (PM) and magnetically ordered states. However, its amplitude appears to be significantly lower in comparison with phononic component. The obtained value of spin fluctuation temperature $θ_{sf}$(MnGe) $\approx$ 330 K correlates well with previous estimations, as well as with results of various experiments, which predict the existence of SFs in MnGe at least up to 250 $-$ 300 K.

AI.tldr: Study analyzes zero-field heat capacity of metallic helimagnet MnGe, identifying electronic, phononic, and spin fluctuation contributions via resistivity decomposition. Found electronic coefficient γ̃ ≈ 7 mJ/mol·K², Debye temperature Θ_D ≈ 350 K, and spin fluctuation temperature θ_sf ≈ 330 K, consistent with prior experiments showing SFs persisting up to 250-300 K.
AI.motivation: To identify and characterize the spin fluctuation (SF) contribution to heat capacity in metallic helimagnet MnGe, which was previously unclear in the literature.
AI.method: Heat capacity analysis combining resistivity decomposition to separate electronic, phononic, and spin fluctuation contributions in MnGe helimagnet across PM and magnetically ordered states.
AI.result: Three heat capacity components were identified: electronic (γ̃ ≈ 7 mJ/mol·K²), phononic (Θ_D ≈ 350 K), and spin fluctuation terms existing in both PM and ordered states. The SF amplitude is smaller than phononic component. The derived θ_sf(MnGe) ≈ 330 K correlates with prior estimations and experimental data showing SFs up to 250-300 K.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully identified three heat capacity contributions in MnGe: electronic (γ ~ 7 mJ/mol·K²), phononic (Θ_D ~ 350 K), and spin fluctuation components. The spin fluctuation temperature θ_sf ≈ 330 K aligns with prior experiments predicting SFs up to 250-300 K.

title: Octahedral Tilting in Halide Double Perovskites: Disentangling Lone-Pair Chemistry and Geometric Effects
summary: Halide double perovskites (HDPs) have emerged as promising alternatives to their lead-based counterparts. However, their structural dynamics is less explored than that of conventional halide perovskites. In this work, we investigate octahedral tilting at 0 K and the relative stability of tetragonal and cubic phases of a set of 57 halide double perovskites (HDPs). By combining structural and energetic descriptors with simple geometric metrics, we identify the main trends controlling the stabilization of one-tilt tetragonal phases across this family. We find that both the magnitude of the tilt angles and the energetic preference for tilted phases correlate primarily with the Goldschmidt tolerance factor $t$. The presence of stereochemically active lone-pair cations also correlates with enhanced tilting; however, this trend largely reflects that lone-pair chemistries in HDPs occur together with ionic sizes that shift $t$ away from unity. Consistent with this picture, we observe several compounds without lone pairs that nonetheless exhibit strong octahedral tilting. Finally, using machine-learned interatomic potentials, we connect the 0 K tilting energetics to finite-temperature behavior: compounds with more strongly stabilized tilt phases exhibit higher transition temperatures, and phonon spectra at 350 K reveal soft and broad modes that are consistent with the trends in tolerance factors, tilt angles, and tilt energies at 0 K. Our results provide a systematic reference for structure-stability relationships in HDPs and clarify when lone-pair chemistry is correlated with, rather than the primary cause of, octahedral tilting.

AI.tldr: This study investigates octahedral tilting and phase stability in 57 halide double perovskites (HDPs) at 0 K and connects these to finite-temperature behavior. Using structural/energetic descriptors and machine-learned potentials, the research identifies tolerance factor t as the primary controller of tilt stabilization, while clarifying that lone-pair effects are secondary to ionic size contributions.
AI.motivation: Halide double perovskites (HDPs) are promising lead-free alternatives to conventional halide perovskites, but their structural dynamics remain underexplored compared to their lead-based counterparts. Understanding octahedral tilting mechanisms and phase stability is crucial for applications.
AI.method: The study combined structural and energetic descriptors with geometric metrics to analyze tetragonal and cubic phases of 57 HDPs at 0 K. Machine-learned interatomic potentials were used to connect 0 K energetics to finite-temperature behavior and calculate phonon spectra at 350 K.
AI.result: The Goldschmidt tolerance factor t is the primary controller of both tilt angle magnitude and energetic preference for tilted phases. Stereochemically active lone-pair cations correlate with enhanced tilting, but this effect largely stems from ionic sizes that shift t away from unity. Several compounds without lone pairs still show strong tilting. Compounds with more strongly stabilized tilt phases exhibit higher transition temperatures, and phonon spectra at 350 K reveal soft/broad modes consistent with tolerance factor trends.
AI.conclusion: Tolerance factor t is the main determinant of octahedral tilting in HDPs, while lone-pair chemistry is correlated with rather than the primary cause of tilting. This provides a systematic reference for structure-stability relationships in HDPs and clarifies the distinction between direct lone-pair effects and indirect ionic size effects.

title: Electronic and Magnonic Properties of $g$-Wave Altermagnetism in Intercalated Transition Metal Dichalcogenides
summary: Altermagnetism is a recently identified class of magnetic order characterized by unconventional momentum-dependent spin splitting in the absence of net magnetization, and understanding its electronic and magnetic properties is essential for revealing its fundamental physics and potential applications. In this work we investigate two intercalated transition-metal dichalcogenides, Fe$_{1/4}$NbS$_2$ and V$_{1/3}$NbS$_2$, as candidate altermagnetic materials by using effective tight-binding and spin models complemented by first-principles calculations. We show that the $g$-wave electronic spin splitting originates from bond-dependent hopping anisotropy, leading to material-dependent nodal structures. For the magnetic excitations, the emergence of chiral splitting in the magnon dispersion is controlled by single-ion anisotropy, which manifests as altermagnetic-like nodal structures when spins are oriented along an easy-axis. Conversely, this altermagnetic signature disappears when the spins are aligned in an easy-plane. Beyond linear spin-wave theory, we find that $1/S$ corrections from magnon--magnon interactions preserve the symmetry and nodal structure of the band splitting while generally reducing its magnitude, with strong antiferromagnetic exchange leading to a non-negligible renormalization of the chiral splitting. Our findings establish intercalated transition-metal dichalcogenides as promising platforms for understanding the interplay between crystal symmetry, non-relativistic spin splitting, and magnetic properties in altermagnets.

AI.tldr: This study investigates Fe₁/₄NbS₂ and V₁/₃NbS₂ as candidate altermagnetic materials, demonstrating that their g-wave electronic spin splitting originates from bond-dependent hopping anisotropy, and magnetic excitations exhibit chiral splitting controlled by single-ion anisotropy that disappears when spins align in an easy-plane.
AI.motivation: Altermagnetism is a recently discovered class of magnetic order featuring momentum-dependent spin splitting without net magnetization. Understanding its electronic and magnetic properties is essential for revealing fundamental physics and exploring potential applications, yet candidate materials needed systematic investigation.
AI.method: Effective tight-binding and spin models complemented by first-principles calculations were employed to study two intercalated transition-metal dichalcogenides (Fe₁/₄NbS₂ and V₁/₃NbS₂), including analysis beyond linear spin-wave theory examining 1/S corrections from magnon-magnon interactions.
AI.result: The g-wave electronic spin splitting originates from bond-dependent hopping anisotropy, producing material-dependent nodal structures. Chiral splitting in magnon dispersion emerges when spins orient along an easy-axis but disappears in easy-plane configuration. Magnon-magnon interactions preserve symmetry and nodal structure while generally reducing splitting magnitude, with strong antiferromagnetic exchange causing non-negligible renormalization.
AI.conclusion: Intercalated transition-metal dichalcogenides emerge as promising platforms for understanding the interplay between crystal symmetry, non-relativistic spin splitting, and magnetic properties in altermagnets, establishing a foundation for further exploration of altermagnetic phenomena.

title: Evaluation of External Magnetic Flux Density in Piezo-Flexomagnetic Nanobeams Using a Hybrid 1D-2D Finite Element Framework
summary: This study numerically evaluates the external magnetic flux density generated in air by the bending of a piezo-flexomagnetic nanobeam. In several classes of non-contact sensors, the magnetic field induced in the surrounding medium is often more useful than the internal magnetic response. However, most theoretical studies on piezo-flexomagnetic nanostructures neglect the external magnetic domain. The proposed framework employs a coupled hybrid finite element formulation combining a 1D Timoshenko beam model with a 2D magnetostatic problem encompassing both the beam body and the surrounding air domain. The formulation is verified against analytical solutions of magnetically isolated piezo-flexomagnetic beams. The results demonstrate the presence of a significant external magnetic flux distribution in free-standing structures, even in the absence of piezomagnetic coupling. A systematic sensitivity analysis further identifies the material parameters most strongly influencing the external transverse magnetic flux density. These findings provide insight into the design of nanoscale non-contact magnetoelastic sensing systems.

AI.tldr: This study develops a numerical framework to evaluate the external magnetic flux density in air generated by piezo-flexomagnetic nanobeams, addressing a gap in existing literature that typically neglects the external magnetic domain. The approach combines 1D Timoshenko beam theory with 2D magnetostatic finite element analysis.
AI.motivation: Most theoretical studies on piezo-flexomagnetic nanostructures focus on internal magnetic response while neglecting the external magnetic domain, which is more useful for non-contact sensor applications. This gap limits the design of effective nanoscale magnetoelastic sensors.
AI.method: A coupled hybrid finite element formulation combining a 1D Timoshenko beam model with a 2D magnetostatic problem encompassing both the beam body and the surrounding air domain. The formulation was verified against analytical solutions of magnetically isolated piezo-flexomagnetic beams.
AI.result: Results demonstrate significant external magnetic flux distribution in free-standing structures, even in the absence of piezomagic coupling. A systematic sensitivity analysis identifies the material parameters most strongly influencing the external transverse magnetic flux density.
AI.conclusion: This work provides valuable insight into the design of nanoscale non-contact magnetoelastic sensing systems by addressing the previously neglected external magnetic domain.

title: Higher-order Weyl nodes driven by helical magnetic order in EuAgAs
summary: Magnetic topological semimetals provide a fertile ground for exploring how long-range magnetic order can alter electronic band structures and generate novel quasiparticles such as Weyl fermions. Here, we investigate the coupled magnetic and electronic structure of single-crystalline EuAgAs, a hexagonal pnictide whose magnetic ground state has remained elusive. Using neutron diffraction and resonant elastic X-ray scattering, we identify an unusual magnetic ordering sequence with two successive phase transitions at $T_\mathrm{N1} = 12$ K and $T_\mathrm{N2} = 8$ K. We observe two slightly different magnetic propagation vectors, one associated with $T_\mathrm{N1}$ and the other with $T_\mathrm{N2}$. Spherical neutron polarimetry reveals that the magnetic structure is a transverse helix aligned along the $c$ axis with a period that is approximately twice the $c$ lattice parameter. First-principles calculations for the helical phase predict subtle band folding effects and the emergence of effective higher-order Weyl nodes. These topological features appear near the calculated Fermi energy $E_{\mathrm{F}}$ which, however, lies above the position of $E_{\mathrm{F}}$ obtained from angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy so could not be probed in this study.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the magnetic and electronic structure of single-crystalline EuAgAs, a hexagonal pnictide, revealing two successive magnetic phase transitions at 12 K and 8 K, with a transverse helix magnetic structure. First-principles calculations predict emergence of higher-order Weyl nodes near the Fermi energy, though experimental verification was limited by discrepancies between calculated and measured Fermi level positions.
AI.motivation: Magnetic topological semimetals offer a platform to explore how long-range magnetic order modifies electronic band structures and generates novel quasiparticles like Weyl fermions. EuAgAs presented an opportunity to investigate such coupling in a hexonal pnictide system whose magnetic ground state was previously unknown.
AI.method: The study combined neutron diffraction, resonant elastic X-ray scattering, spherical neutron polarimetry, first-principles calculations, and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy to investigate the magnetic structure and electronic properties of single-crystalline EuAgAs.
AI.result: Two successive magnetic phase transitions were identified at T_N1 = 12 K and T_N2 = 8 K, each associated with different magnetic propagation vectors. The ground state magnetic structure was determined to be a transverse helix aligned along the c-axis with a period approximately twice the c lattice parameter. First-principles calculations predicted band folding effects and higher-order Weyl nodes near the calculated Fermi energy, though these features could not be probed experimentally due to the Fermi energy lying below the calculated position.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals an unusual magnetic ordering sequence in EuAgAs with two distinct phase transitions and predicts topological features including higher-order Weyl nodes emerging from the helical magnetic order. However, the discrepancy between calculated and experimentally measured Fermi energies prevented direct observation of these topological features.

title: Zero-net-magnetization hybrid magnet
summary: Zero-net-magnetization magnets possess ultradense and ultrafast application potential, benefiting from their intrinsic zero stray field and terahertz dynamics characteristics. Herein, we propose the concept of zero-net-magnetization hybrid magnet, in which magnetic atoms with opposite spin polarization are partially coupled via spatial inversion ($P$) symmetry, partially via rotation/mirror ($C/M$) symmetry or partially without any symmetry correlation. From a local perspective and neglecting the interactions between local regions, hybrid magnet can be regarded as being composed of $PT$-antiferromagnet (possessing the combined symmetry ($PT$) of $P$ and time-reversal ($T$)), altermagnet, or fully compensated ferrimagnet. To realize hybrid magnet, we propose that such system can be constructed by forming heterojunction with three types of zero-net-magnetization magnetic monolayers. We mainly investigate the heterojunction composed of two kinds of zero-net-magnetization magnets, among which one type corresponds to fully compensated ferrimagnet. When heterojunction hybrid magnet exhibits a type-II band alignment, only one of electron doping and hole doping can induce a net magnetic moment, while the other hardly generates any net magnetization. Taking the heterojunction constructed by $PT$-antiferromagnet and fully compensated ferrimagnet as an example, we verify our proposal by means of the tight-binding (TB) model. Finally, taking the $\mathrm{Cr_2C_2S_6}$/$\mathrm{CrMoC_2S_6}$ heterojunction as an example, we perform first-principles calculations combined with electric field modulation to validate our TB model and theoretical proposal.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new concept of "zero-net-magnetization hybrid magnets" combining magnetic atoms with opposite spin polarization via different symmetry operations (spatial inversion P, rotation/mirror C/M, or no symmetry). These hybrid systems can be constructed via heterojunctions of zero-net-magnetization magnetic monolayers, and show unique property that in type-II band alignment, only one type of carrier doping (electron OR hole) can induce net magnetization.
AI.motivation: Zero-net-magnetization magnets offer ultradense and ultrafast application potential due to their intrinsic zero stray field and terahertz dynamics characteristics. Previous work lacked systematic approach to design and understand these materials, particularly how carrier doping affects their magnetic properties - a critical knowledge gap for applications.
AI.method: The authors employed: (1) Theoretical symmetry analysis to classify hybrid magnets into PT-antiferromagnet, altermagnet, and fully compensated ferrimagnet categories; (2) Tight-binding (TB) model calculations on PT-antiferromagnet/fully compensated ferrimagnet heterojunction for preliminary validation; (3) First-principles calculations on Cr2C2S6/CrMoC2S6 heterojunction combined with electric field modulation for further verification.
AI.result: The key finding is that heterojunction hybrid magnets with type-II band alignment exhibit unidirectional magnetization response to carrier doping - only electron doping or hole doping (not both) can induce a net magnetic moment. This was verified both theoretically with TB model and computationally with DFT calculations on the proposed material system.
AI.conclusion: The concept of zero-net-magnetization hybrid magnets provides a new paradigm for designing magnetic materials with controllable magnetization through external stimuli (carrier doping/electric field). The unidirectional doping response enables novel device applications where magnetic state can be switched by changing doping type, with potential for ultradense memory and terahertz-speed devices.

title: Green's Function-Free Formalism of Projective Truncation Approximation
summary: In previous works, the projected truncation approximation (PTA) was developed as a systematic and controlled method to truncate the equation of motion of Green's functions (GFs) for a given quantum or classical many-body Hamiltonian. The static averages are obtained self-consistently with the GF through the spectral theorem. In this work, PTA is reformulated as a self-consistent theory for the reduced density matrices (RDMs) without reference to GF. We separately discuss the issues of determining the dynamical matrix ${\bf M}$ and solving the physical quantities from it. The properties of ${\bf M}$ is clarified and the solution of PTA equations is cast into an over-constrained optimization problem. This makes connection of the present theory to the variational RDM theory. We discuss various issues of PTA under this formalism, including the scheme of alternative inner product, the generalized virial theorem, the generalized Wick's theorem, and the static component problem of PTA.

AI.tldr: This paper reformulates the Projected Truncation Approximation (PTA), previously developed for Green's functions, as a self-consistent theory for reduced density matrices without referencing Green's functions. The dynamical matrix M is clarified, and the solution is cast into an over-constrained optimization problem, connecting the theory to variational RDM methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to reformulate PTA for reduced density matrices (RDMs) without reference to Green's functions, clarify the properties of the dynamical matrix M, and establish connections to variational RDM theory. Previous formulations relied on Green's functions, and this work aims to provide a more direct approach to truncated many-body problems.
AI.method: PTA is reformulated as a self-consistent theory for RDMs by treating the solution of PTA equations as an over-constrained optimization problem. The method involves determining the dynamical matrix M, solving for physical quantities from M, and discussing alternative inner product schemes. Generalized virial theorem and generalized Wick's theorem are derived within this formalism.
AI.result: The properties of the dynamical matrix M are clarified, and the solution method is established through an over-constrained optimization framework. The theory connects to variational RDM methods. Various theoretical issues are addressed, including the scheme of alternative inner product, generalized virial theorem, generalized Wick's theorem, and the static component problem of PTA.
AI.conclusion: PTA has been successfully reformulated as a self-consistent theory for RDMs without reference to Green's functions. The connection to variational RDM theory is established through the optimization problem formulation. This work extends the theoretical framework of PTA and provides a clearer understanding of the dynamical matrix properties while offering new perspectives on many-body truncated equations.

title: High-Throughput Bayesian Optimization of Cement-Salt Hydrates Composites for Seasonal Thermochemical Energy Storage
summary: Thermochemical energy storage (TCES) based on salt hydrates is a promising route for seasonal heat storage; however, the design of practical sorbent materials remains challenging due to a non-trivial coupling between composition, synthesis feasibility, performance, and cost. Here, focusing on salt-into-matrix cement-based composites, we demonstrate that a high-throughput experimental framework based on Bayesian optimization (BO) can be used to orchestrate the optimization process of composite materials for low-temperature TCES. The explored design space is defined by salt type, salt concentration, water-to-cement ratio, and additive-to-cement ratio, while two competing objectives are pursued in parallel, namely the specific energy and the specific energy cost. The BO-guided campaign identified Pareto-optimal composites based on CaCl$_2$, Zn(NO$_3$)$_2$, and LiCl, highlighting the promise of cement-salt combinations that have been only marginally explored, or not previously reported, in cement-based TCES systems. The best-performing formulation (LiCl-based), achieved an average specific energy of about $\SI{458}{\kilo\joule\per\kilo\gram}$, whereas CaCl$_2$- and Zn(NO$_3$)$_2$-based composites showed lower but still competitive specific energy values combined with more favorable specific energy cost. Overall, the optimized formulations improved the specific energy of previously developed cement-based materials by up to a factor of five, although it remains below that of state-of-the-art composites based on silica gel and expanded vermiculite. Nonetheless, the present materials, notably CaCl$_2$- and Zn(NO$_3$)$_2$-based composites, offer an attractive cost-to-performance balance, highlighting BO as an effective strategy for accelerated TCES materials discovery.

AI.tldr: This study demonstrates Bayesian optimization as an effective high-throughput approach for discovering optimized cement-based composite materials for thermochemical energy storage (TCES). Using CaCl₂, Zn(NO₃)₂, and LiCl salts in a cement matrix, the research achieved up to 5x improvement in specific energy over prior cement-based materials, with LiCl-based composites showing the highest energy density (~458 kJ/kg) while CaCl₂ and Zn(NO₃)₂ variants offered better cost-to-performance balances.
AI.motivation: Thermochemical energy storage using salt hydrates offers promise for seasonal heat storage, but designing practical sorbent materials is challenging due to complex interdependencies between composition, synthesis feasibility, performance, and cost. Previous approaches relied on trial-and-error or limited screening, necessitating a more efficient optimization strategy.
AI.method: A Bayesian optimization (BO) framework was employed toorchestrate the high-throughput optimization of salt-into-matrix cement-based composites. The design space included four variables: salt type, salt concentration, water-to-cement ratio, and additive-to-cement ratio. Two competing objectives—specific energy and specific energy cost—were simultaneously optimized in parallel to identify Pareto-optimal formulations.
AI.result: The BO-guided investigation identified Pareto-optimal composites using three salts: CaCl₂, Zn(NO₃)₂, and LiCl. The LiCl-based formulation achieved the highest average specific energy of ~458 kJ/kg. CaCl₂ and Zn(NO₃)₂-based composites showed lower but competitive specific energies combined with more favorable specific energy costs. Overall, the optimized formulations improved specific energy by up to 5x compared to previously developed cement-based materials, though they remained below state-of-the-art silica gel and expanded vermiculite composites. Notably, CaCl₂ and Zn(NO₃)₂-based materials offered particularly attractive cost-to-performance balances.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian optimization proved effective for accelerated discovery of TCES materials, successfully identifying promising cement-salt combinations that had been only marginally explored or not previously reported in cement-based TCES systems. While further improvements are needed to match silica gel/expanded vermiculite performance, the present work establishes BO as a valuable strategy for navigating complex materials design spaces with multiple competing objectives.

title: Fractonic Constraints and Magnetic Order in a Dipole-Conserving Spin Chain
summary: This work investigates the competition between dipole conservation, which imposes strong dynamical constraints and prevents the propagation of isolated spin excitations, and Ising-type interactions that favor ordering. Specifically, we explore the ground state phase diagram of a one-dimensional spin chain in the presence of both fractonic constraints and interactions. Despite the kinetic constraints, the system stabilizes an antiferromagnetic dipole-ordered ground state, where the ordering occurs at the level of spin pairs rather than individual spins. At a large Ising interaction strength, the model undergoes a phase transition from a dipole-ordered phase to a spin antiferromagnetic phase. In contrast, for ferromagnetic Ising interactions, the model exhibits both antiferromagnetic and ferromagnetic dipole ordered phases. At sufficiently large negative interaction strength, the dipole ordered phase transitions to a ferromagnetic phase with conventional spin ferromagnetic order. To characterize these distinct phases, we employ density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) simulations alongside large-scale diagonalization. We analyze appropriate order parameters, along with features of the entanglement spectrum and dynamical spectral functions. In limiting cases, the observed transitions can be understood by mapping the dipole conserving model onto effective XXZ models in a restricted Hilbert space of composite spins.

AI.tldr: Study exploresground state phase diagram of 1D spin chain with fractonic dipole conservation constraints and Ising interactions. DMRG reveals multiple phases: dipole-ordered (AFM/FM), spin AFM, and conventional FM, stabilized by competition between kinetic constraints and interaction strength.
AI.motivation: This work addresses the competition between dipole conservation (fractonic constraints preventing isolated spin excitation propagation) and Ising-type interactions favoring magnetic ordering in 1D spin chains. The study aims to understand how these conflicting dynamical constraints influence ground state phase behavior.
AI.method: The researchers employed density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) simulations combined with large-scale diagonalization. They analyzed order parameters, entanglement spectra, and dynamical spectral functions. Effective XXZ models in restricted Hilbert spaces were used to understand limiting case transitions.
AI.result: The system stabilizes an antiferromagnetic dipole-ordered ground state at the level of spin pairs. A phase transition occurs from dipole-ordered to spin antiferromagnetic phase at large Ising interaction strength. For ferromagnetic Ising interactions, both antiferromagnetic and ferromagnetic dipole ordered phases emerge. At large negative interaction strength, dipole ordered phase transitions to conventional spin ferromagnetic phase.
AI.conclusion: The competition between dipole conservation and Ising interactions produces rich phase diagrams including dipole-ordered phases (antiferromagnetic and ferromagnetic variants), spin antiferromagnetic phase, and conventional ferromagnetic phase. The dipole constraints stabilize pair-based ordering instead of individual spin ordering.

title: Nanoscale Thermal Imaging of Dislocation-Mediated Heat Transport
summary: Dislocations in crystalline materials are widely exploited to tailor the thermal conductivity of semiconductors and thermoelectrics, yet a critical gap persists: direct measurement of local thermal resistance at individual buried dislocations, along with its spatial extent, remains elusive due to the limitations of conventional thermal probes. Here, we use in situ scanning transmission electron microscopy-electron energy-loss spectroscopy to map nanoscale temperature distributions across a low-angle SrTiO3 grain boundary with periodic dislocation arrays. Our results reveal a temperature drop of 47 K across the dislocation array. The associated temperature-field distortions are concentrated near the dislocation cores, consistent with stronger local thermal resistance at these discrete sites rather than a uniformly distributed resistance along the array. We further identify a distinct two-scale heat transport characteristic near the dislocation array: core-dominated effects over approximately 4.8-6.2 nm and extended inter-core influences over approximately 10.3-14.3 nm. Atomic-scale structural and vibrational analyses further reveal core-associated atomic reconstruction and localized optical-phonon perturbations, providing a microscopic basis for the stronger local thermal resistance inferred near dislocation cores. These findings quantitatively resolve spatial heterogeneity of dislocation-mediated heat transport, uncover its atomic-scale mechanism, and provide a quantitative basis for defect engineering, guiding the design of high-performance thermoelectrics, semiconductors, high-temperature structural alloys, and other functional crystalline materials.

AI.tldr: This study uses in situ STEM-EELS to directly measure nanoscale temperature distributions across a low-angle SrTiO3 grain boundary with periodic dislocation arrays, revealing a 47 K temperature drop concentrated near dislocation cores with two-scale heat transport characteristics.
AI.motivation: A critical gap existed in directly measuring local thermal resistance at individual buried dislocations due to limitations of conventional thermal probes. Understanding dislocation-mediated heat transport is important for engineering high-performance thermoelectrics, semiconductors, and high-temperature structural alloys.
AI.method: In situ scanning transmission electron microscopy-electron energy-loss spectroscopy (STEM-EELS) was used to map nanoscale temperature distributions across a low-angle SrTiO3 grain boundary with periodic dislocation arrays. Atomic-scale structural and vibrational analyses were also performed.
AI.result: A temperature drop of 47 K was measured across the dislocation array. Temperature-field distortions were concentrated near dislocation cores (4.8-6.2 nm), with extended inter-core influences (10.3-14.3 nm). Core-associated atomic reconstruction and localized optical-phonon perturbations provided a microscopic basis for stronger local thermal resistance.
AI.conclusion: The findings quantitatively resolve spatial heterogeneity of dislocation-mediated heat transport and uncover its atomic-scale mechanism, providing a quantitative basis for defect engineering to guide the design of high-performance thermoelectrics, semiconductors, high-temperature structural alloys, and other functional crystalline materials.

title: Filling-Sensitive Spectral Complexity from Hilbert-Space Holonomy in Fragmented Non-Hermitian Systems
summary: We show that Hilbert-space holonomy provides a geometric organizing principle for spectral reality in fragmented non-Hermitian many-body systems, complementary to conventional symmetry protection. In two minimal fragmented models, complex spectra can arise only within the most symmetric sectors: half filling in the fermion model and zero magnetization in the spin chain. Adding or removing a single particle, or flipping a single spin, renders the spectra entirely real despite unchanged periodic boundary conditions, reminiscent of boundary-condition sensitivity in systems with a non-Hermitian skin effect. We explain this by viewing nonreciprocal hopping amplitudes as a discrete gauge field on the Krylov graph: trivial holonomy permits a diagonal similarity transformation to the Hermitian limit, whereas nontrivial holonomy obstructs it and allows complex spectra. In certain regimes, trivial holonomy admits an emergent-boundary interpretation, and longer-range models exhibit finite real and complex regions governed by the same criterion.

AI.tldr: This paper explores Hilbert-space holonomy as an organizing principle for spectral properties in fragmented non-Hermitian many-body systems. Complex spectra emerge only in the most symmetric sectors (half-filling for fermions, zero magnetization for spins), while breaking symmetry (adding/removing particles or flipping spins) yields entirely real spectra. The mechanism is explained via discrete gauge fields on the Krylov graph, where trivial holonomy permits diagonalization to Hermitian form, while nontrivial holonomy blocks this and allows complex spectra.
AI.motivation: Understanding spectral properties in non-Hermitian many-body systems is challenging because conventional symmetry protection fails to explain why complex spectra appear only in symmetric sectors. The authors seek a geometric principle beyond symmetry to organize spectral reality in fragmented systems.
AI.method: The study uses two minimal fragmented models (a fermion model and a spin chain) analyzed via Hilbert-space holonomy. The authors map nonreciprocal hopping amplitudes to a discrete gauge field on the Krylov graph, examining holonomy's triviality and its relation to similarity transformations achieving Hermitian limits.
