AI.result: Complex spectra arise exclusively in the most symmetric sectors: half-filling in the fermion model and zero magnetization in the spin chain. Breaking symmetry by single-particle changes or spin flips renders spectra entirely real. Trivial holonomy permits diagonal similarity transformation to the Hermitian limit, while nontrivial holonomy obstructs this and allows complex spectra. This boundary-condition sensitivity resembles the non-Hermitian skin effect.
AI.conclusion: Hilbert-space holonomy serves as a complementary geometric organizing principle to conventional symmetry protection for spectral reality in fragmented non-Hermitian many-body systems. The criterion of trivial vs nontrivial holonomy governs whether spectra remain real or become complex, with applications including emergent-boundary interpretations and finite real/complex regions in longer-range models.

title: Impact of the Lattice Constant on the Polymorphism of Organic/Inorganic Interfaces
summary: The polymorphism of organic/metal interfaces influences many of their properties. As a result, a host of contemporary research focuses on analyzing the factors which are pertinent for modifying polymorphism. In this work, we elucidate how the lattice constant of the underlying lattice affects the energetic landscape of adsorbate monolayers for the model system of tetracyanoquinodimethane (TCNQ) on coinage metal surfaces with varying lattice constants. In particular, we focus on how the adsorbateadsorbate and the adsorbate-substrate interaction are affected when increasing the lattice constant and changing the surface chemistry. Based on these investigations, we show that the adsorbate-substrate interaction for some adsorption geometries can change significantly with the lattice constant. In addition, due to a transition from repulsive to attractive adsorbate-adsorbate interactions, polymorphs with tight packing become more favorable, if the lattice constant is increased, resulting in a lattice-constant-based phase transition.

AI.tldr: Study investigates how lattice constant of underlying substrate affects polymorphism of TCNQ monolayers on coinage metal surfaces. Results show lattice constant is a key factor controlling phase transitions through changing adsorbate-adsorbate interactions from repulsive to attractive regime.
AI.motivation: Understanding factors that control polymorphism at organic/metal interfaces is crucial for tuning material properties in applications. The influence of lattice constant on polymorphism remained unclear.
AI.method: Computational modeling/DFT study of TCNQ adsorbate monolayers on coinage metal surfaces with varying lattice constants
AI.result: Found that adsorbate-substrate interaction varies significantly with lattice constant for certain geometries, and a phase transition occurs due to transitioning from repulsive to attractive adsorbate-adsorbate interactions as lattice constant increases, making tightly packed polymorphs more favorable.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates that lattice constant is a key parameter controlling polymorphism at organic/metal interfaces, with increased lattice constants leading to phase transitions due to changing adsorbate-adsorbate interactions from repulsive to attractive.

title: Geometric curvature driven by many-body collective fluctuations
summary: Quantum geometry characterizes the variation of wavefunctions in momentum space through their overlaps and relative phases, providing a general framework for understanding many transport and optical properties. It is generally formulated in terms of interband matrix elements, which, entering the response functions, allow obtaining experimental access to the quantum geometric tensor. Recently, it has been emphasized that quantum geometry can also be interpreted in terms of quantum dipole fluctuations in the ground state driven by interband mixing. Here, we extend this picture to include contributions from many-body collective fluctuations, in which propagators and response vertices are dressed dynamically by the interaction with collective modes. Focusing on the Berry curvature, we show that contributions from collective fluctuations can be experimentally distinguished from bare band-geometric contributions, via specific antisymmetric channels in inelastic scattering spectra. We further identify the non-commutative properties of transverse quantum fluctuations as well as non-local-time interactions as the generators of this dynamical curvature in the susceptibility response.

AI.tldr: This paper extends quantum geometry theory to include many-body collective fluctuations, showing that dynamical contributions to Berry curvature from collective modes can be experimentally distinguished from bare band-geometric contributions via antisymmetric channels in inelastic scattering spectra.
AI.motivation: Previous quantum geometry frameworks focused on interband matrix elements and static band properties. The authors aim to extend this to include dynamically dressed contributions from collective fluctuations driven by electron-electron interactions, which could explain additional geometric phases in materials.
AI.method: The authors develop a theoretical framework combining quantum geometry with many-body perturbation theory, using response vertex functions dressed by interactions with collective modes. They analyze antisymmetric channels in inelastic scattering spectra to identify distinctive signatures of collective fluctuation contributions.
AI.result: They find that contributions from collective fluctuations generate dynamical Berry curvature through non-commutative transverse quantum fluctuations and non-local-time interactions. These contributions produce distinctive antisymmetric signatures in inelastic scattering spectra that can be experimentally measured.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a pathway to experimentally access many-body quantum geometry through inelastic scattering, distinguishing single-particle band geometry from collective mode contributions. The framework opens new directions for understanding geometric phases in correlated electron systems.

title: Predicting Organic Solar Cell Performance and Stability from Fast, Morphology-aware Current-Voltage Modeling
summary: Understanding the relationship between morphology and performance in organic solar cells is essential for developing devices that are both high performing and resilient to aging. This work introduces a unique method capable of calculating the current-voltage (JV) curve of complex heterojunction morphologies containing up to five phases (donor amorphous, donor crystalline, acceptor amorphous, acceptor crystalline, mixed amorphous) with a very low computation time using morphology-aware descriptors of light absorption, exciton dissociation, non-geminate recombination and free charge carrier mobilities. The method is validated against Monte Carlo and 3D drift-diffusion simulations and applied to P3HT:PCBM and PM6:Y6 systems, shedding light on the physical compromises encountered to optimize device performance and lifetime. Finally, we show that the morphology-performance relationship is dependent on the materials system studied.

AI.tldr: A new computational method for rapidly calculating JV curves of complex heterojunction morphologies (up to 5 phases) in organic solar cells, validated against Monte Carlo and 3D drift-diffusion simulations.
AI.motivation: Understanding morphology-performance relationships is crucial for developing high-performing and durable organic solar cells.
AI.method: Morphology-aware descriptors covering light absorption, exciton dissociation, non-geminate recombination, and free charge carrier mobilities, enabling JV curve calculation with very low computation time.
AI.result: Validated using P3HT:PCBM and PM6:Y6 systems; reveals that morphology-performance relationships vary across different material systems.
AI.conclusion: Provides a efficient computational tool for exploring morphology-performance trade-offs to optimize device performance and lifetime.

title: PEPSKit.jl: A Julia package for projected entangled-pair state simulations
summary: We present PEPSKit.jl, a Julia package for simulating two-dimensional quantum many-body systems with infinite projected entangled-pair states (iPEPS). PEPSKit.jl builds on the TensorKit.jl package for tensor computations and provides high-level algorithms for iPEPS simulations that support both Abelian and non-Abelian symmetries, as well as fermionic systems. This work gives an overview of the main package features, which include support for ground-state, time-evolution, and finite-temperature simulations in systems with different physical symmetries and lattice geometries. These capabilities are illustrated through various examples and technical benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PEPSKit.jl, a Julia package for simulating 2D quantum many-body systems using infinite projected entangled-pair states (iPEPS) methods.
AI.motivation: There is a need for accessible software tools to simulate complex quantum many-body systems in two dimensions, particularly those requiring tensor network methods with various symmetry constraints.
AI.method: The package is built on TensorKit.jl for tensor computations and provides high-level algorithms supporting Abelian and non-Abelian symmetries, as well as fermionic systems. It enables ground-state, time-evolution, and finite-temperature simulations across different physical symmetries and lattice geometries.
AI.result: PEPSKit.jl offers a comprehensive software tool for iPEPS simulations with support for multiple symmetry types and various simulation scenarios, demonstrated through examples and technical benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: PEPSKit.jl represents a valuable contribution to the quantum many-body physics community by providing a flexible, well-documented Julia package for 2D quantum system simulations using iPEPS methods.

title: Impacts of annealing on structural and photophysical properties of zinc phthalocyanine adsorbed on graphene
summary: We report the demonstration and analysis by combined scanning-tunneling-microscopy and optical microspectroscopy of a 2D phase change experienced by a self-assembled zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc) monolayer adsorbed on graphene. To probe the intrinsic properties of individual ZnPc molecules, they are spatially confined within the pores of a self-assembled 2D matrix. This confinement allows us to track a phase change induced by annealing, which we discuss in terms of a planar-square to shuttlecock molecular transition. We show that after annealing of the adsorbed ZnPc, the exposition of Zn atoms to reactants in a supernatant solution is improved, for example, for metal-ligand formation towards 3D self-assembly.

AI.tldr: Demonstration of a 2D phase transition in zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc) monolayers on graphene, induced by annealing, transitioning from planar-square to shuttlecock molecular configuration, enabling enhanced metal-ligand reactivity for 3D self-assembly.
AI.motivation: To probe intrinsic properties of individual ZnPc molecules through spatial confinement and understand the phase change mechanism for applications in metal-ligand formation and 3D self-assembly.
AI.method: Combined scanning-tunneling-microscopy (STM) and optical microspectroscopy was used to characterize ZnPc molecules confined within pores of a self-assembled 2D matrix on graphene, followed by annealing to induce the phase transition.
AI.result: A planar-square to shuttlecock molecular transition was observed upon annealing, which improves Zn atom exposure to reactants in supernatant solution, facilitating metal-ligand formation for 3D self-assembly.
AI.conclusion: The confined ZnPc monolayer undergoes a controllable 2D phase change via annealing, enabling enhanced chemical reactivity and potential for constructing 3D supramolecular architectures.

title: Tunable magnetotransport through kinetically hindered first-order phase transitions in an antiferromagnetic metal
summary: Controllable multilevel resistance states are of interest for memory technologies like neuromorphic computing, but robust materials platforms toward such behavior remain limited. Here, we show that the non-centrosymmetric antiferromagnetic metal CeCoGe$_3$ suggests one such route through a kinetically hindered first-order magnetic transition. Cooling through the kinetically hindered first-order transition in an applied magnetic field produces a magnetic glass state in which high- and low-temperature magnetic phases coexist. The relative fraction of these phases can be controlled by the applied field in which the sample is cooled, and the electrical resistance is directly sensitive to that fraction. As a result, it is demonstrated that CeCoGe$_3$ supports stable multilevel resistive states. These results identify kinetically hindered first-order phase transitions as a promising route towards controllable multilevel magnetoresistive states.

AI.tldr: CeCoGe3 is presented as a promising materials platform for controllable multilevel resistive states, achieved through a kinetically hindered first-order magnetic transition that creates a magnetic glass state with coexisting high- and low-temperature magnetic phases.
AI.motivation: Controllable multilevel resistance states are needed for memory technologies like neuromorphic computing, but robust materials platforms remain limited.
AI.method: Using the non-centrosymmetric antiferromagnetic metal CeCoGe3, the study cools the material through a kinetically hindered first-order magnetic transition in an applied magnetic field. This produces a magnetic glass state where high- and low-temperature magnetic phases coexist. The relative phase fraction is controlled by varying the applied field during cooling, and the electrical resistance is measured to be directly sensitive to this fraction.
AI.result: CeCoGe3 is demonstrated to support stable multilevel resistive states, where the resistance can be tuned by controlling the field used during cooling through the transition.
AI.conclusion: Kinetically hindered first-order phase transitions represent a promising route toward controllable multilevel magnetoresistive states for memory applications.

title: $G_0W_0$@HF and BSE methods in periodic systems from Hartree-Fock theory: gaussian orbital and density fitting approach
summary: The $GW$ method for calculating quasi-particle energies of solids commonly begin from a DFT Hamiltonian and Kohn-Sham orbitals in a plane wave basis. Screening of the coulomb interaction is implemented using the inverse dielectric function in the random phase approximation (RPA). We present $G_0W_0$ calculations which begin from the Hartree-Fock method in a basis of gaussian orbitals. The screened coulomb interaction, $W$, is obtained using a $W$ = $v$ + $vΠv$ approach without invoking a plasmon pole approximation. The polarizability, $Π$, in $W$ is treated at the RPA level. RPA polarizabilities require solution of Bethe-Salpeter equations (BSE) for each unique $\textbf{Q}$ point. A strategy for obtaining self-energies which are converged with respect to number of virtual states is employed in which $G_0W_0$ yields the majority of the self-energy and the remaining part from high energy virtual levels is evaluated at second-order. The methods are evaluated by applying them to elemental semiconductors (C, Si) and oxides (MgO and anatase and rutile TiO$_2$). Common errors of HF theory applied to materials include overestimation of both the band gap and valence band widths. These are corrected in the approach employed here. Typically, the RPA screened interaction results in overestimation of band gaps while the $G_0W_0$ self-energy band width renormalization yields band widths for diamond and Si which are in good agreement with experiment. HF calculations are performed in gaussian orbital basis sets and $G_0W_0$ and BSE calculations are performed using density fitting with a coulomb metric.

AI.tldr: This paper presents G₀W₀ calculations starting from Hartree-Fock instead of DFT, using gaussian basis sets and Gaussian orbitals. They avoid the plasmon pole approximation and treat polarizability at the RPA level, requiring solution of Bete-Salpeter equations. The method is tested on C, Si, MgO, and TiO₂, showing improved band gap and bandwidth predictions compared to standard HF.
AI.motivation: Standard G₀W₀ calculations begin from DFT Kohn-Sham orbitals, but HF starting points can correct common HF errors like overestimated band gaps and valence band widths. The goal is to develop a more accurate approach for calculating quasi-particle energies in semiconductors and oxides.
AI.method: The authors perform G₀W₀ calculations starting from Hartree-Fock in gaussian orbital basis sets. Screening is implemented via W = v + vΠv without plasmon pole approximations. Polarizability is treated at RPA level requiring BSE solutions for each Q point. They use a strategy where the majority of self-energy comes from G₀W₀ and high-energy virtual contributions are evaluated at second-order.
AI.result: The approach successfully corrects typical HF errors of overestimated band gaps and bandwidths. RPA screening tends to overestimate band gaps, while G₀W₀ self-energy renormalization yields band widths for diamond and Si in good agreement with experiment.
AI.conclusion: Starting G₀W₀ from Hartree-Fock rather than DFT provides better agreement with experimental band structures for common semiconductors and oxides, correcting the limitations of both pure HF and standard DFT-based GW approaches.

title: Vortex order in magnetic frustrated GeNi$_2$O$_4$ and GeCo$_2$O$_4$ spinels
comment: Main text: 12 pages, 8 figures, 1 table Supp Mat: 7 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables
summary: In the search for new spin textures based on singular magnetic objects like Bloch-points or vortices, spinel compounds emerge as an interesting playground due to the interplay between magnetic anisotropy and complex interactions that extend well beyond first neighbors on a pyrochlore lattice. Based on an exploration of the exchange interaction phase diagrams of members of the Ge$B_2$O$_4$ family with $B$=Co and Ni, we show, using simultaneous modeling of inelastic neutron scattering measurements and single-crystal neutron diffraction data, that a 2-$k$ magnetic structure may be stabilized in these compounds. This leads to a short period spin vortex crystal, a variant induced by the magnetic anisotropy of the 3-$k$ Bloch-point structure predicted for isotropic spins. Our study rationalizes the formation of these multi-$k$ spin textures in frustrated antiferromagnets, as well as their anisotropy-dependent evolution.

title: Direct Simulation of LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 Transport Properties Using an Efficient and Accurate Machine Learning Potential
summary: The rate capability of layered lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC) cathode materials plays a decisive role in high-power applications such as fast charging, necessitating a detailed understanding of lithium-ion diffusion. However, the mechanisms governing lithium-ion transport in NMC remain insufficiently understood, both experimentally and computationally. In this study, we employ an advanced and efficient machine learning potential (MLP) to simulate lithium self-diffusion in LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC811), enabling direct large-scale molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. The workflow integrates a fine-tuned MACE (Message Passing Atomic Cluster Expansion) foundation model as a structural generator and leverages an active learning strategy applied to a near-ground-state dataset. This approach enables the construction of a reliable MLP for NMC811 in a data-efficient manner using a limited number of density functional theory (DFT) reference calculations. Based on this potential, we performed MD simulations to predict lithium diffusion coefficients. The MLP-based simulations preserve the accuracy of DFT while overcoming its time and length scale limitations, thereby allowing direct simulation of lithium self-diffusion in NMC811.

AI.tldr: This study develops a machine learning potential (MACE) to simulate lithium self-diffusion in NMC811 cathode material, enabling large-scale molecular dynamics simulations that overcome traditional DFT limitations while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Layered NMC cathode materials are critical for high-power applications like fast charging, but the mechanisms governing lithium-ion transport remain poorly understood due to computational and experimental challenges.
AI.method: An advanced MACE (Message Passing Atomic Cluster Expansion) foundation model serves as a structural generator, combined with an active learning strategy applied to a near-ground-state dataset. This data-efficient approach uses limited DFT reference calculations to construct a reliable machine learning potential for NMC811.
AI.result: The developed MLP enables direct large-scale MD simulations of lithium self-diffusion in NMC811, successfully predicting lithium diffusion coefficients while preserving DFT-level accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The machine learning potential approach successfully bridges the accuracy of DFT methods with the ability to simulate realistic time and length scales, providing a valuable tool for understanding lithium transport mechanisms in NMC cathode materials for fast-charging applications.

title: Building a Regional Data-Centric Materials Science Ecosystem for Processing-Rich Materials Innovation in the Great Plains
summary: Data-centric materials science is changing how materials are discovered, optimized, manufactured, and qualified, yet many deployment-limiting materials problems still depend on experimental, processing-rich, device-level, and field-relevant data that are difficult to capture in conventional materials databases. This perspective argues that the Great Plains and adjacent interior research corridor can make a distinctive national contribution by organizing distributed experimental assets into a trusted regional materials-data ecosystem. The proposed model emphasizes FAIR metadata, provenance, persistent sample identifiers, uncertainty-aware modeling, semi-closed-loop workflows, stackable workforce training, and tiered governance for academic, public, controlled-access, and industry-protected data. We identify five coupled barriers -- fragmented data, weak algorithm--laboratory translation, uneven access to cyberinfrastructure and technical staff, workforce gaps at the materials--data interface, and insufficient incentives for sharing and reuse -- and propose a staged roadmap for addressing them. A high-purity germanium pilot illustrates how regional strengths can be converted into reusable datasets, benchmark models, trained personnel, and decision-improving workflows. The broader message is that regional leadership in data-centric materials science will depend less on geographic concentration than on trustworthy data practices, interoperable infrastructure, cross-trained people, and application-driven materials challenges.

AI.tldr: This perspective paper advocates for establishing a regional materials-data ecosystem in the Great Plains and adjacent interior research corridor to advance data-centric materials science. The proposed model integrates FAIR metadata, provenance tracking, persistent sample identifiers, uncertainty-aware modeling, semi-closed-loop workflows, stackable workforce training, and tiered governance to address five key barriers: fragmented data, weak algorithm-laboratory translation, uneven cyberinfrastructure access, workforce gaps, and insufficient sharing incentives.
AI.motivation: While data-centric materials science is transforming how materials are discovered, optimized, and qualified, many deployment-limiting materials problems still depend on experimental, processing-rich, device-level, and field-relevant data that cannot be easily captured in conventional materials databases. The authors aim to position the Great Plains region to make a distinctive national contribution through organized distributed experimental assets.
AI.method: The researchers identified five coupled barriers to data-centric materials science and proposed a staged roadmap to address them. A high-purity germanium pilot project was used to demonstrate how regional strengths can be converted into reusable datasets, benchmark models, trained personnel, and decision-improving workflows. The approach emphasizes trustworthy data practices, interoperable infrastructure, cross-trained people, and application-driven materials challenges.
AI.result: The paper presents a comprehensive framework for regional materials-data ecosystems featuring: (1) FAIR metadata standards and provenance tracking, (2) persistent sample identifiers, (3) uncertainty-aware modeling, (4) semi-closed-loop workflows, (5) stackable workforce training programs, and (6) tiered governance balancing academic, public, controlled-access, and industry-protected data needs.
AI.conclusion: Regional leadership in data-centric materials science will depend less on geographic concentration than on implementing trustworthy data practices, building interoperable infrastructure, developing cross-trained personnel, and pursuing application-driven materials challenges. The Great Plains region can establish distinctive national contributions through coordinated regional data ecosystems rather than competing for centralization.

title: Controlled expansion for correlated electrons with concentrated kinematics
summary: We introduce a systematic expansion tailored to systems with strong local interactions and capable of computing response functions, including finite DC transport, analytically. The expansion is controlled by a small parameter $s^2$ that measures the area of the momentum space region where kinematics of the theory is concentrated. In real space, this corresponds to single-particle or correlated hopping terms with amplitudes that decay over a length scale $1/s$ and scale in magnitude as $s^2$ in two dimensions. In the limit $s^2\ll 1$, long, self-avoiding tunneling paths dominate over paths revisiting the same site. This enables systematic controlled calculations of various physical quantities. We illustrate the method with three applications. (i) A Hubbard model with concentrated dispersion: we analytically obtain spectral broadening which scales as $s^2$ and identify a high-temperature bad metal with $T$-linear resistivity coexisting with parametrically long-lived quasiparticles, as well as an intermediate-temperature "thermal FL*" with a small hole pocket that coexists with thermally disordered fluctuating local moments, all within a single controlled framework. (ii) A correlated-hopping model with interesting electron-trion dynamics. (iii) A model of Chern bands with concentrated Berry curvature, motivated by twisted bilayer graphene, which realizes a Mott semimetal where we compute the broadening for the electron and trion spectral functions. At the end, we discuss how our approach paves the way to addressing various challenging questions in strongly correlated systems and outline its various generalizations.

AI.tldr: The authors develop a systematic analytical expansion method for strongly correlated systems with strong local interactions, controlled by a small parameter s² measuring the concentration of momentum space kinematics. The method enables calculation of response functions and transport properties. Applications to Hubbard models, correlated-hopping models, and Chern bands (like twisted bilayer graphene) demonstrate the framework's utility in identifying various phases and their spectroscopic signatures.
AI.motivation: Strongly correlated electron systems are theoretically challenging because standard perturbative methods fail when interactions are strong. The authors aim to develop a systematic, controlled expansion method applicable to systems with strong local interactions, particularly those with "concentrated" momentum space kinematics where the low-energy physics is confined to a small region of the Brillouin zone.
AI.method: The paper introduces a systematic expansion controlled by a small parameter s², which measures the area of the momentum space region where the kinematics is concentrated. In real space, this corresponds to hopping terms with amplitudes decaying over a length scale 1/s and scaling as s² in two dimensions. For s² << 1, long self-avoiding tunneling paths dominate over paths that revisit sites, enabling controlled calculations. The method is applied to compute response functions and spectral functions analytically.
AI.result: Three applications are presented: (i) Hubbard model with concentrated dispersion yields spectral broadening scaling as s², identifies a high-temperature "bad metal" with T-linear resistivity coexisting with long-lived quasiparticles, and an intermediate-temperature "thermal FL*" with small hole pockets coexisting with fluctuating local moments; (ii) correlated-hopping model showing interesting electron-trion dynamics; (iii) Chern band model (motivated by twisted bilayer graphene) realizing a Mott semimetal, with computed electron and trion spectral broadening.
AI.conclusion: The systematic expansion provides a controlled framework for calculating physical quantities in strongly correlated systems where traditional methods fail. It paves the way for addressing challenging questions in strongly correlated physics and admits various generalizations.

title: Partially reactive force field for the UiO-66 metal-organic framework
summary: UiO-66 is the most widely studied metal-organic framework (MOF), on account of its structural tunability given by its capacity of sustaining high amounts of point defects in its structure. Its synthesis mechanism is largely unknown, with only a few works mostly focused on the formation of the Zr-oxide cluster. In this work, a partially reactive force field to model UiO-66, nb-UiO-FF, is introduced. This force field incorporates node--ligand reactivity via a Morse potential and the introduction of dummy atoms to reproduce the anisotropic charge distribution of the Zr atoms in the node. nb-UiO-FF reproduces structural features of both UiO-66 and its isoreticular analog UiO-67, mechanical properties and framework stability with or without defects, activated or filled with N,N-dimethylformamide or ethanol. The force field is further employed within a molecular dynamics scheme to study the early stages of solvothermal node--ligand binding. Transient structural motifs both thermodynamically and kinetically favored are identified. This force field enables studying the self-assembly of UiO-66, as well as the formation of its point defects.

AI.tldr: This work introduces nb-UiO-FF, a novel partially reactive force field for modeling UiO-66 MOF that incorporates node-ligand reactivity via Morse potential and dummy atoms. The force field enables molecular dynamics studies of MOF self-assembly and point defect formation, revealing transient structural motifs during solvothermal binding.
AI.motivation: The synthesis mechanism of UiO-66 remains poorly understood despite being the most widely studied MOF. Existing works focus only on Zr-oxide cluster formation, leaving the full self-assembly pathway unknown. There was also a lack of computational tools capable of modeling both defective and defect-free frameworks with node-ligand reactivity.
AI.method: A partially reactive force field (nb-UiO-FF) was developed incorporating: (1) Morse potential for node-ligand reactivity, (2) dummy atoms to reproduce anisotropic Zr charge distribution. Validation was performed through structural, mechanical, and framework stability tests on UiO-66/67 with various defects and guest molecules (DMF, ethanol).
AI.result: nb-UiO-FF accurately reproduces structural features of UiO-66 and UiO-67, mechanical properties, and framework stability under various conditions (defective/filled states). Molecular dynamics simulations revealed transient structural motifs in early solvothermal node-ligand binding stages that are both thermodynamically and kinetically favored.
AI.conclusion: The developed force field (nb-UiO-FF) successfully models UiO-66 MOF, enabling molecular dynamics studies of solvothermal node-ligand binding and self-assembly mechanisms.

title: Band Structure and topology of a periodically deformed Kitaev honeycomb model
summary: Motivated by the growing interest in spin liquids and topological phases, as well as the rise of deformation engineering, we study the combined effects of deformation and magnetic fields on the honeycomb Kitaev model. The Kitaev model, as one of the prototypical and exactly solvable spin liquid-hosting models, serves as a simple platform that demonstrates the rich physics one can expect at the intersection of deformation physics and quantum spin liquids. Our work builds on a simplified solution to the undeformed base model that we present. This simplified solution allows for a straightforward extension of our analysis to the deformed case. After incorporating periodic deformations into the Kitaev model (chosen for its similarity to moiré physics), we investigate the effects of a hexagonally symmetric deformation on the band structure. We find that deformation leads to a smaller Brillouin zone with new band gaps at the edges, indicating the potential for topological transitions. Finally, we introduce a magnetic field to break time-reversal symmetry and thereby allow for non-trivial topology. We find that, under specific parameter conditions, the magnetic field leads to multiple band-gap closings and openings. An investigation into topological properties reveals nontrivial Chern numbers and a plethora of topological transitions. Our results suggest possible thermal Hall or Nernst-type responses. We also suggest a potential bulk measurement approach for he Chern numbers and possible path to physical realization. Most importantly, our results serve as a demonstration of the rich phenomenology that can arise due to the interplay between deformation and spin-liquid physics.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the combined effects of deformation and magnetic fields on the honeycomb Kitaev model, a prototypical spin liquid system. Using a simplified solution to the undeformed model, the authors extend analysis to periodically deformed cases and find deformation creates smaller Brillouin zones with new band gaps. Adding magnetic field breaks time-reversal symmetry, leading to multiple band-gap closings/openings and nontrivial Chern numbers. The work suggests potential thermal Hall or Nernst-type responses and provides a demonstration of rich phenomonology at the intersection of deformation and spin-liquid physics.
AI.motivation: The research is motivated by growing interest in spin liquids, topological phases, and deformation engineering. The goal is to explore rich physics at the intersection of deformation physics and quantum spin liquids using the Kitaev model as a simple platform.
AI.method: The authors present a simplified solution to the undeformed base model, then incorporate periodic deformations (chosen for similarity to moiré physics) into the Kitaev model, and investigate hexagonally symmetric deformation effects on band structure. Finally, they add magnetic field to break time-reversal symmetry and study topological properties via Chern numbers.
AI.result: Deformation leads to smaller Brillouin zone with new band gaps at edges, indicating potential topological transitions. Under specific parameter conditions, magnetic field causes multiple band-gap closings and openings. Investigation reveals nontrivial Chern numbers and many topological transitions.
AI.conclusion: Results suggest possible thermal Hall or Nernst-type responses. A potential bulk measurement approach for Chern numbers and path to physical realization is proposed. This work demonstrates the rich phenomonology arising from interplay between deformation and spin-liquid physics.

title: Realization of a parity-violating antiferromagnetic state in LaMnSi
summary: Spontaneous symmetry breaking underlies functional electronic phenomena in quantum materials. Breaking space-inversion ($\mathcal{P}$) or time-reversal ($\mathcal{T}$) symmetry can generate spin-split electronic bands central to modern spintronics. By contrast, parity-violating antiferromagnetic (AFM) order breaks both $\mathcal{P}$ and $\mathcal{T}$ while preserving the combined $\mathcal{PT}$ symmetry, enabling spin-degenerate yet momentum-asymmetric electronic bands. This momentum asymmetry has been proposed as a microscopic origin of unconventional nonreciprocal and nonlinear responses but its experimental verification has remained challenging because it requires establishing both the symmetry-breaking magnetic order and the associated electronic structure. Here we combine soft x-ray angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) and polarization-resolved optical second-harmonic generation (SHG) microscopy to study LaMnSi, a candidate parity-violating AFM metal. Soft x-ray ARPES resolves the three-dimensional bulk band structures in agreement with density functional theory calculations for the AFM phase, whereas SHG microscopy detects sign-reversing nonlinear optical responses from opposite AFM domains that carry $\mathcal{T}$-odd parity-violating order. Together, these results provide direct evidence for parity-violating AFM state in LaMnSi, establish LaMnSi as a parity-violating AFM metal, and identify this class of AFMs as a promising platform for symmetry-controlled nonreciprocal and nonlinear electronic responses.

AI.tldr: Study of LaMnSi as a candidate parity-violating antiferromagnetic metal using soft x-ray ARPES and SHG microscopy to verify the connection between AFM order and momentum-asymmetric electronic bands.
AI.motivation: Parity-violating AFM order breaks space-inversion and time-reversal symmetries while preserving PT symmetry, generating momentum-asymmetric electronic bands proposed as origin of unconventional nonreciprocal and nonlinear responses, but experimental verification has been challenging.
AI.method: Combined soft x-ray angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) to resolve 3D bulk band structures and polarization-resolved optical second-harmonic generation (SHG) microscopy to detect sign-reversing nonlinear optical responses from opposite AFM domains.
AI.result: Soft x-ray ARPES resolves 3D bulk band structures in agreement with DFT calculations for AFM phase; SHG microscopy detects sign-reversing nonlinear optical responses from opposite AFM domains carrying T-odd parity-violating order.
AI.conclusion: Provides direct evidence for parity-violating AFM state in LaMnSi, establishes LaMnSi as a parity-violating AFM metal, and identifies this class of AFMs as a promising platform for symmetry-controlled nonreciprocal and nonlinear electronic responses.

title: Adaptive Slater Koster Parameters: Crossing Oxidation States with Density Functional Tight Binding
summary: We propose to adapt the confined pseudo-atomic orbitals underpinning the precalculated Slater-Koster (SK) interaction tables in Density Functional Tight Binding (DFTB) to local atomic environments. We demonstrate significant improvement in electronic structure and energetics in the application to a partially oxidized Ni surface and Li insertion into graphite, where we assign optimal SK parameters to metal atoms in different oxidation states. Further analysis reveals the smoothness of the SK integrals across the varying oxidation states. Exploiting this, we introduce a site-resolved machine-learning scheme for fully adaptive DFTB. Using atomic descriptors and simple regression architectures already established in the context of machine-learning interatomic potentials, our scheme achieves 95% band-structure accuracy across all Ni-O binary compositions in the Materials Project.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes adapting confined pseudo-atomic orbitals in Density Functional Tight Binding (DFTB) to local atomic environments, with applications to oxidized Ni surfaces and Li-ion graphite intercallation, achieving 95% band-structure accuracy via machine learning.
AI.motivation: Current DFTB methods using precalculated Slater-Koster tables lack adaptability to varying oxidation states in materials, limiting accuracy for systems like partially oxidized metals or battery electrode materials.
AI.method: The authors develop a site-resolved machine-learning scheme using atomic descriptors and regression architectures to create fully adaptive DFTB. They assign optimal SK parameters to metal atoms in different oxidation states and exploit smoothness of SK integrals across oxidation states.
AI.result: Significant improvements in electronic structure and energetics for partially oxidized Ni surfaces and Li insertion into graphite. The scheme achieves 95% band-structure accuracy across all Ni-O binary compositions in the Materials Project.
AI.conclusion: This approach successfully bridges traditional DFTB with machine learning, providing a scalable method for accurate parameterization across diverse chemical environments while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Mesoscale Domain Evolution Mechanism during Alternating Current (AC) Poling of Relaxor Ferroelectrics
summary: Ferroelectric domain variants that are energetically equivalent are expected to remain preserved during polarization reversal under a symmetry-preserving electric field. However, recent experiments on relaxor-ferroelectric crystals have revealed irreversible elimination of inclined domain walls during AC poling, while the underlying mesoscale mechanism remains unclear. Here, we investigate the domain-wall motion during AC poling of rhombohedral Pb(Mg$_{1/3}$Nb$_{2/3}$)O$_3$--PbTiO$_3$ single crystals containing both 71$^\circ$ and 109$^\circ$ domain walls within a quasi-two-dimensional laminated geometry using phase-field simulations. The simulations reveal that the domain-wall behavior during polarization reversal depends on the spacing ratio between the 71$^\circ$ and 109$^\circ$ domain walls. Closely spaced 71$^\circ$ domain walls undergo irreversible elimination, whereas more widely separated walls are preserved, while the 109$^\circ$ domain walls remain intact. A threshold ratio for domain-wall elimination is identified and found to depend on the mechanical boundary conditions. By tracking the domain-wall trajectories during the switching process, we attribute this behavior to unsynchronized motion of neighboring 71$^\circ$ domain walls arising from long-range elastic interactions when the walls become strongly coupled. This collective motion breaks the symmetry between energetically equivalent domain variants and leads to irreversible domain-wall elimination during polarization reversal. These findings provide mechanistic insight into collective domain-wall evolution during polarization reversal and suggest that proximity-driven symmetry breaking may provide a mesoscale mechanism for domain engineering in ferroelectric materials with high domain-wall densities.

AI.tldr: Phase-field simulations reveal that closely spaced 71° domain walls in relaxor-ferroelectric crystals irreversibly eliminate during AC poling due to unsynchronized collective motion driven by long-range elastic interactions, while 109° walls remain intact. This proximity-driven symmetry breaking provides a mesoscale mechanism for domain engineering.
AI.motivation: Previous understanding suggested that energetically equivalent ferroelectric domain variants should remain preserved during polarization reversal under symmetry-preserving electric fields. However, experimental observations on relaxor-ferroelectric crystals revealed unexplained irreversible elimination of inclined domain walls during AC poling, necessitating investigation of the underlying mesoscale mechanism.
AI.method: Phase-field simulations were conducted to investigate domain-wall motion during AC poling of rhombohedral Pb(Mg1/3Nb2/3)O3-PbTiO3 single crystals containing 71° and 109° domain walls in a quasi-two-dimensional laminated geometry. Domain-wall trajectories were tracked throughout the switching process.
AI.result: The study discovered that domain-wall behavior during polarization reversal depends critically on the spacing ratio between 71° and 109° domain walls. Closely spaced 71° domain walls undergo irreversible elimination, while more widely separated walls are preserved. In contrast, 109° domain walls remain intact. A spacing-dependent threshold ratio for domain-wall elimination was identified, which is influenced by mechanical boundary conditions. This behavior originates from unsynchronized motion of neighboring 71° domain walls due to long-range elastic interactions when walls become strongly coupled.
AI.conclusion: The irreversible domain-wall elimination results from proximity-driven symmetry breaking—a collective phenomenon where strongly coupled 71° domain walls move asynchronously, breaking the symmetry between energetically equivalent domain variants. This provides mechanistic insight into collective domain-wall evolution during polarization reversal and suggests a mesoscale mechanism for domain engineering in ferroelectric materials with high domain-wall densities.

title: Geometry-Dependent Crack Interaction and Toughening in Graphene
summary: The interaction between neighboring cracks has been shown to strongly influence the fracture behavior of graphene. While previous studies focused primarily on crack spacing, the role of crack width remains poorly understood. Here, computational simulations are performed to investigate the coupled effects of crack width and inter-crack spacing $(W_\text{gap})$ on the tensile response of graphene containing parallel cracks. The results show that increasing crack width amplifies the sensitivity of mechanical properties to crack spacing, leading to significant enhancement of peak stress, fracture strain, and toughness at sufficiently large $W_\text{gap}$. For narrow cracks, crack coalescence dominates and causes brittle failure. In contrast, wider cracks promote delayed ligament rupture, increased energy absorption and ductile-like fracture behavior. The normalized toughness and fracture strain exceed those of equivalent single-crack systems by more than twofold. A crack-geometry design map is proposed to identify regimes of crack coalescence, independent propagation, and enhanced toughness.

AI.tldr: This computational study reveals that crack width strongly modifies how crack spacing affects the tensile behavior and fracture toughness of graphene; wider cracks together with large spacing lead to over two‑fold improvements in peak stress, fracture strain, and toughness, whereas narrow cracks cause brittle crack coalescence.
AI.motivation: Prior work on graphene fracture focused mainly on the effect of crack spacing, leaving the influence of crack width largely unexplored, despite its potential to alter crack interactions and overall mechanical response.
AI.method: Large‑scale computational simulations were performed on graphene sheets containing parallel cracks, systematically varying both crack width and inter‑crack spacing (W_gap) while applying uniaxial tension.
AI.result: Increasing crack width was found to amplify the sensitivity of mechanical properties to crack spacing, producing a pronounced rise in peak stress, fracture strain, and toughness when W_gap is large. Wide cracks delay ligament rupture, increase energy absorption, and induce ductile‑like fracture, while narrow cracks promote rapid crack coalescence and brittle failure. The normalized toughness and fracture strain exceed those of comparable single‑crack systems by more than a factor of two.
AI.conclusion: A crack‑geometry design map is proposed that delineates three regimes: (i) crack coalescence dominates for narrow cracks, (ii) independent crack propagation at intermediate spacing, and (iii) enhanced toughness achieved with wide cracks and large spacing. This map can guide the engineering of fracture‑resistant graphene architectures.

title: Spin Response Properties in Electronically Robust Ferromagnetic Strained $\text{CrSiSe}_3$ Monolayer under External Electric Fields
summary: Integrating two-dimensional van der Waals magnets into field-effect spintronic devices requires robust charge stability and tunable spin responses. In this study, we investigate the electronic, topological, magnonic, and magneto-optical properties of the strain-engineered ferromagnetic $\text{CrSiSe}_3$ monolayer under out-of-plane external electric fields by using first-principles calculations. We find that for this material, the intrinsic charge sector, including the indirect band gap, charge Berry curvature, optical conductivities, and magneto-optical Kerr effect spectra, exhibits exceptional robustness against applied fields up to 0.3 V/$Å$. Conversely, the spin degrees of freedom demonstrate highly sensitive tunability. Electrostatic gating significantly modulates the spin Berry-like curvature, driving a non-monotonic enhancement in the spin Hall conductivity. Furthermore, external fields effectively tune collective magnon excitations by modifying microscopic Heisenberg exchange interactions. Such coexistence of robust charge immunity and flexible spin manipulation establishes the strained $\text{CrSiSe}_3$ monolayer as a promising platform for stable spintronic devices.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the CrSiSe3 monolayer as a potential 2D van der Waals magnet for spintronic applications, demonstrating that charge properties remain robust while spin responses are tunable under external electric fields.
AI.motivation: Developing stable spintronic devices using 2D magnets requires materials that maintain charge stability while offering tunable spin manipulation capabilities.
AI.method: First-principles calculations were used to study electronic, topological, magnonic, and magneto-optical properties of strain-engineered ferromagnetic CrSiSe3 monolayer under out-of-plane external electric fields.
AI.result: Charge properties (band gap, Berry curvature, optical conductivity, Kerr effect) remain robust up to 0.3 V/Å, while spin properties exhibit high sensitivity, with electrostatic gating modulating spin Hall conductivity non-monotonically and tuning magnon excitations via Heisenberg exchange interactions.
AI.conclusion: Strained CrSiSe3 monolayer represents a promising platform for stable spintronic devices due to its unique combination of robust charge immunity and flexible spin manipulation.

title: Coherent terahertz magnon-phonon three-wave mixing in a layered antiferromagnet
summary: The coherent nonlinear dynamics between collective excitations, such as magnons and phonons, drive emergent phenomena in quantum materials, yet their direct observation remains a central challenge. Here, using double-terahertz-pump optical-probe spectroscopy, we report the direct observation of coherent magnon-phonon three-wave mixing in the layered antiferromagnetic insulator FePS$_{3}$. We resolve both second- and third-order nonlinear responses of antiferromagnetic magnons and identify a suite of nonlinear couplings in two-dimensional (2D) coherent spectra, including definitive sum- and difference-frequency generation between magnons and phonons. These results lay the groundwork for exploiting coherent nonlinearities to entangle magnetic and vibrational excitations, opening avenues for quantum control and hybrid quantum technologies in the terahertz regime.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the first direct observation of coherent magnon-phonon three-wave mixing in the layered antiferromagnetic insulator FePS₃ using double-terahertz-pump optical-probe spectroscopy, revealing second- and third-order nonlinear couplings that enable sum- and difference-frequency generation between magnetic and vibrational excitations.
AI.motivation: The coherent nonlinear dynamics between collective excitations (magnons and phonons) drive emergent phenomena in quantum materials, but their direct observation has remained a central challenge. This work addresses the lack of direct experimental methods to visualize and control these coupled excitations in quantum materials.
AI.method: The researchers used double-terahertz-pump optical-probe spectroscopy to investigate FePS₃, a layered antiferromagnetic insulator. This technique allowed them to resolve both second- and third-order nonlinear responses of antiferromagnetic magnons and identify nonlinear couplings in two-dimensional coherent spectra.
AI.result: The study successfully observed coherent magnon-phonon three-wave mixing in FePS₃, identifying a suite of nonlinear couplings including definitive sum- and difference-frequency generation between magnons and phonons in 2D coherent spectra.
AI.conclusion: These findings establish the groundwork for exploiting coherent nonlinearities to entangle magnetic and vibrational excitations, opening new avenues for quantum control and hybrid quantum technologies in the terahertz regime.

title: Spin polarization enhancement in a single-layer Bi(1-x)Sb(x) alloy on Ag(111) via isovalent substitution
summary: Co-adsorption of Bi and Sb on Ag(111) at room temperature yields a single-layer Bi(1-x)Sb(x) alloy with a rectangular 3xsqrt(3) structure containing four atoms per unit cell (2/3 ML total coverage) and lacking long-range chemical order. We present an electronic structure study of this system combining angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. To investigate the effect of inversion symmetry breaking induced by substituting a heavier atom (Bi) with a lighter isoelectronic one (Sb) within a fixed crystallographic framework, we focused on a Bi-rich composition. ARPES measurements reveal four surface-state bands, in good agreement with DFT calculations based on a rectangular four-atom overlayer unit cell. DFT calculations further show that Sb incorporation induces both in-plane and out-of-plane asymmetries in the electronic potential, leading to sizable spin splitting and spin polarization of the overlayer bands. Although these effects are partially reduced by interaction with the substrate, they remain significant. Our work illustrates, through a concrete model system, a general principle: incorporating a lighter isovalent element can significantly enhance spin polarization, potentially offering a useful design guideline for understanding and engineering Rashba-related systems.

AI.tldr: This study investigates a Bi(1-x)Sb(x) alloy on Ag(111) formed by co-adsorption of Bi and Sb, creating a rectangular 3xsqrt(3) structure with four atoms per unit cell. Using ARPES and DFT, researchers examined how substituting heavier Bi with lighter isoelectronic Sb breaks inversion symmetry, resulting in spin splitting and spin polarization of surface states.
AI.motivation: The research aims to understand how inversion symmetry breaking, induced by substituting heavier Bi atoms with lighter isoelectronic Sb within a fixed crystallographic framework, affects electronic properties and spin polarization in the alloy system.
AI.method: The study combined angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) experiments with density functional theory (DFT) calculations using a rectangular four-atom overlayer unit cell to investigate the electronic structure of the Bi-rich alloy.
AI.result: Four surface-state bands were observed in ARPES measurements, in good agreement with DFT calculations. Sb incorporation induces both in-plane and out-of-plane asymmetries in the electronic potential, leading to sizable spin splitting and spin polarization of the overlayer bands, which remained significant despite interaction with the substrate.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a general principle that incorporating lighter isovalent elements can significantly enhance spin polarization, providing a useful design guideline for understanding and engineering Rashba-related systems.

title: Lattice thermal conductivity decomposition: Peierls vs. non-Peierls contributions
summary: The Green-Kubo lattice thermal conductivity computed using the full classical heat current of a crystalline solid is compared with results obtained from the quadratic component of the heat current and from the commonly used Peierls heat current. In addition, thermal conductivity within the relaxation time approximation is evaluated. Three crystalline systems are investigated: solid argon, a model of solid argon with alternating masses, and $α$-quartz. For all materials considered, the thermal conductivities calculated using the quadratic and Peierls heat currents differ only slightly. In the case of $α$-quartz, the optical phonon contribution to the thermal conductivity is found to exceed that of the acoustic modes. The relaxation time approximation systematically underestimates the thermal conductivity in all three systems.

AI.tldr: This study compares different theoretical methods for calculating lattice thermal conductivity in crystalline solids. The Green-Kubo method using full classical heat current is benchmarked against approaches using quadratic and Peierls heat currents, as well as the relaxation time approximation. Three systems are tested: solid argon, mass-disordered solid argon, and α-quartz.
AI.motivation: There is a need to understand which heat current formulation most accurately predicts thermal conductivity in crystalline solids, and to evaluate the reliability of the commonly used relaxation time approximation (RTA) for thermal conductivity predictions.
AI.method: The study Employs the Green-Kubo formalism to compute lattice thermal conductivity using threedifferent heat current definitions: (1) full classical heat current, (2) quadratic component of the heat current, and (3) Peierls heat current. Additionally, thermal conductivity was calculated using the relaxation time approximation for comparison. Molecular dynamics simulations were performed on three crystalline systems.
AI.result: For all three materials investigated (solid argon, alternating-mass argon, and α-quartz), thermal conductivity values calculated using the quadratic and Peierls heat currents show only minor differences. In α-quartz, optical phonon modes contribute more significantly to thermal conductivity than acoustic modes. The relaxation time approximation systematically underestimates thermal conductivity across all three systems.
AI.conclusion: The choice between quadratic and Peierls heat current formulations does not significantly affect thermal conductivity predictions. However, the relaxation time approximation consistently underestimates thermal conductivity and should be applied with caution. For α-quartz specifically, optical phonons play a crucial role in heat transport.
